Gap summary (1 sentence): Defines and operationalizes procedure-oriented fairness via explanation-quality gap metrics (ΔREF/ΔVEF) and proposes CFA to jointly optimize utility, traditional group fairness, and explanation fairness, but it relies on design choices like top-K thresholds, fidelity and explainer selection (which may vary across domains) and does not specify deployment-time monitoring or governance for explanation fairness over time.
Measurement procedure: Compute explanations via an explainer (GraphLime used in experiments; with neighbors for non-graph data). Compute explanation quality using fidelity (accuracy-based) as prediction change when masking top important feature(s). Compute ΔREF using top-K thresholded quality labels and ΔVEF using mean fidelity within each subgroup’s top-K. Train CFA by learning representations for original features and masked features, minimizing distance between subgroup representations (conditional on utility label) for both original and masked inputs while optimizing utility loss; tune λ based on validation overall score; evaluate over multiple runs with means/std.
Metrics used: Traditional (result-oriented): ΔSP, ΔEO. Procedure-oriented: ΔREF, ΔVEF computed from explanation quality (fidelity). Utility: AUC, F1, accuracy. Also defines an overall score aggregating utility and fairness terms; uses representation-distance metrics (e.g., Sliced-Wasserstein, cosine, KL, MSE) in ablations.
Protected attrs / groups: Sensitive/protected attributes considered in framing and experiments include gender, age, and race; experiments use datasets where s is a binary sensitive attribute (and discuss multi-class extension via pairwise subgroup comparisons/variance).
QA notes: AAAI 2023 paper explicitly distinguishes result-oriented vs procedure-oriented bias, proposes two explanation fairness metrics based on explanation quality (fidelity/top-K) and a distance-based optimization (CFA) over representations of original and attribution-masked inputs. Strong operationalization and evaluation across multiple datasets; limited stakeholder/governance discussion and no monitoring/revision process beyond hyperparameter tuning.
SOC snippet: Motivates societal discrimination risks from biased ML decisions and argues that procedure-oriented bias matters for fair decision making; primarily treats fairness as group-based parity across sensitive attributes (e.g., gender/race/age) and emphasizes transparency via explainability, but does not deeply engage with stakeholder governance or recourse.
Spec snippet: Defines procedure-oriented group fairness via explanation-quality gaps: ΔREF = |P( q̂=1|s=0 ) − P( q̂=1|s=1 )| with q̂ derived from top-K explanation-quality instances, and ΔVEF as the difference in average explanation quality among each subgroup’s top-K instances. Uses fidelity-based explanation quality (prediction change under masking top-k important features) and defines CFA objective L = L_u + λ L_exp, where L_exp minimizes representation distances between sensitive groups for both original and masked inputs; evaluates alongside traditional ΔSP and ΔEO.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other
Threshold value: Top-K criterion for high-quality explanations (K% of instances), and masking top-k important features (often k=1 in experiments) for fidelity; λ controls trade-off between utility and fairness.
Validation snippet: Extensive offline experiments on benchmark tabular datasets (German, Recidivism, Math, Por) comparing CFA to baselines (MLP, Reduction, Reweight) on utility, traditional fairness (ΔSP/ΔEO), and explanation fairness (ΔREF/ΔVEF), plus a graph-domain extension (NIFTY/FairVGNN) and distance-metric ablations; reports overall score improvements and Pareto-style trade-offs.

DES snippet: We propose NeuronFair ... quantitatively interprets DNNs’ fairness violations ... and uses the interpretation results to guide the generation of more diverse instances ... and can handle both structured and unstructured data.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): NeuronFair operationalizes fairness as individual discrimination (IDIs) and proposes an interpretable white-box testing pipeline (ActDiff/AS curve/AUC, biased-neuron-guided generation) validated on multiple datasets, but does not provide lifecycle monitoring or revision mechanisms beyond pre-deployment testing.
Measurement procedure: Compute neuron ActDiff between paired instances differing only in sensitive attribute; plot AS curve (percentage of neurons above ActDiff thresholds) and compute AUC; identify biased layer and biased neurons using threshold Td at intersection of AS curve with y=x; generate IDIs in global+local phases guided by biased neurons with dynamic loss and momentum; evaluate on 7 datasets and compare with Aequitas, SymbGen, ADF, EIDIG.
Metrics used: Individual discrimination via IDI determination (Def.1: prediction differs when only sensitive attributes differ). Discrimination metric: AUC area under AS curve based on neuron activation difference (ActDiff) across layers.
Protected attrs / groups: Gender, race, age (Adult); gender, age (German Credit, Bank Marketing); race (COMPAS); gender (MEPS); gender, race (CelebA-IN, LFW-IN face detection).
QA notes: From abstract+method+evaluation excerpts: proposes interpretable white-box fairness testing for DNNs by identifying biased neurons; defines individual discrimination/IDI; introduces ActDiff/AS curve/AUC metric; evaluates generation effectiveness/efficiency/diversity and fairness improvement via retraining across 7 datasets (structured + image). Threats to validity discuss attribute correlation, single sensitive attribute at a time, and white-box access assumptions.
Spec snippet: Individual discrimination exists when two valid inputs which differ only in the sensitive attributes but receive a different prediction result from a given DNN ...
Threshold value: ActDiff threshold interval step=0.005; discrimination threshold Td defined as AS curve intersection with y=x (example Td=0.33); activation threshold for 'activated neurons' > 0.5 in biased neuron coverage eval; image step_size_g=0.15; other hyperparameters per Table 2.
Validation snippet: Extensive evaluations across 7 datasets and the corresponding DNNs demonstrate NeuronFair’s superior performance ... compared with Aequitas, SymbGen, ADF, and EIDIG.

Citation: Raji, Inioluwa Deborah, I. Elizabeth Kumar, Aaron Horowitz, and Andrew D. Selbst. “The Fallacy of AI Functionality.” In Proceedings of the 2022 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT ’22), June 21–24, 2022, Seoul, Republic of Korea. ACM. https://doi.org/10.1145/3531146.3533158
Failure modes: Other
Fairness notions: Contestability/recourse, Contextual/legal compliance, Other, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a cross-domain taxonomy of AI ‘dysfunction’ and argues that basic functional performance should be treated as a first-order accountability requirement, but does not operationalize this into testable requirements, metrics/thresholds, or concrete lifecycle monitoring/revision procedures.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual + literature-based analysis; develops a taxonomy of AI dysfunction by systematically reviewing case studies using the AIAAIC/AAAIRC repository and iterative tagging.
Metrics used: Not a metrics paper. Discusses functional performance/evaluation and ‘fitness for intended use’ conceptually; no formal fairness metric/threshold specified.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses harms disproportionately affecting minoritized groups (e.g., darker-skinned women in facial recognition; Black and low-income patients); no fixed protected attribute list for a specific system.
QA notes: Conceptual/policy + socio-technical analysis. Strong: clearly stated thesis (functionality as prior question), detailed taxonomy + methodology (case study filtering + iterative tagging), rich cross-domain examples, and concrete legal/organizational intervention discussion. Weak for extraction goals: does not define measurable metrics/thresholds or acceptance criteria; no implemented system evaluation; monitoring/revision not specified as an operational process.
Spec snippet: “We argue that functionality is a meaningful AI policy challenge, operating as a necessary first step towards protecting affected communities from algorithmic harm.”
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Org leadership, Regulators, Users
Validation snippet: Discusses evaluation/validation failures in ML generally and argues for stronger assessment, but does not present or test a concrete system or evaluation protocol in this paper.

DES snippet: Presents REVISE, a Jupyter-notebook-based tool that takes a visual dataset and annotations as input and outputs bias-related metrics and suggested actions across three axes: object-based, person-based, and geography-based analyses; runs locally for privacy and can generate a static PDF summary report; includes tests/scripts to validate dataset formatting.
ENV snippet: Assumes visual datasets encode under- and mis-representation that may not be apparent until after deployment, and that dataset bias can stem from object frequency/scale/context/diversity, demographic portrayal, and geographic representation; also assumes external annotations and pretrained models used for auditing can introduce their own biases and must be interpreted critically.
Failure modes: Other, Unstable subgroup definitions
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a pre-deployment, dataset-centric auditing tool that operationalizes many bias dimensions in visual datasets and suggests mitigation actions, but leaves normative judgments and governance of interventions to users and does not define monitoring/revision processes for deployed pipelines.
Measurement procedure: Run REVISE locally on a dataset with available annotations (object labels, boxes, sensitive-attribute labels, geolocation, tags/captions). Tool computes a suite of metrics by axis (object/person/geography) and surfaces statistically significant anomalies; may incorporate external pretrained models/tools (e.g., scene classifier, language detection) and notes these introduce confounds. Provides ranked actionable augmentation/cleanup suggestions (e.g., pairwise query terms; reconcile duplicate labels; remove dubious gender labels).
Metrics used: Dataset-bias audit metrics across object/person/geography dimensions (frequency, scale, context/co-occurrence, scene diversity, appearance diversity; demographic prominence and contextual representation; geography distribution and correlations with tags/language/income/weather). Uses statistical significance tests (e.g., permutation tests, regression trend tests) to surface anomalous patterns. Focus is dataset-level bias rather than model outcome parity.
Protected attrs / groups: Gender (binary perceived); skin tone (Fitzpatrick 1–6, ordinal); geography/region/country; language-as-proxy for locals vs tourists; income and weather as external region-level attributes (BDD100K case study).
QA notes: REVISE (IJCV 2022) is a dataset-bias auditing and mitigation-support tool for CV datasets, covering object/person/geography bias dimensions with statistically grounded metrics and actionable data-collection/cleanup suggestions. Strong operationalization and empirical audits on COCO/OpenImages/YFCC100m/BDD100K, plus clear limitations about normative bias judgments and risks of demographic labeling; no stakeholder elicitation or lifecycle monitoring/governance plan.
SOC snippet: Emphasizes that determining which surfaced patterns are problematic is a normative judgment that requires cultural and historical context; discusses contested nature of “bias”, risks of reifying gender/skin-tone categories, and potential societal harms from biased datasets; recommends clearer annotation guidance and removal of questionable demographic labels (e.g., gender when person is too small/no face).
Spec snippet: Defines a catalog of measurable dataset-bias metrics and statistical tests (e.g., counts, co-occurrence, scale bins, entropy-based scene diversity, feature-space appearance diversity; demographic prominence/context measures; geography distribution and correlations; permutation tests / regression trend tests) and positions these as a structured audit that can be run pre-deployment on datasets.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Users
Validation snippet: Validates usefulness by applying REVISE to multiple widely used CV datasets (COCO, OpenImages, YFCC100m, BDD100K) and reporting example insights and mitigation suggestions; includes statistical testing to identify significant representation differences.

DES snippet: Defines and analyzes model multiplicity (many equally accurate models with different internals or predictions), distinguishing procedural multiplicity (different decision processes/decision surfaces, possibly with identical predictions) and predictive multiplicity (different predictions for some individuals). Proposes a practical selection process using a documented meta-rule (constraints beyond accuracy, including fairness/recourse) and aggregation techniques (mode aggregation, randomized predictions, random model selection) to reduce arbitrariness and improve justifiability.
ENV snippet: Assumes standard supervised ML deployment settings where model developers face many design choices (features, model class, hyperparameters, random seeds, training data) and typically select models by maximizing predictive accuracy on available data; assumes high-stakes decision contexts like lending where legal and institutional expectations require justification and non-discrimination.
Elicitation snippet: Proposes a deliberative process where model developers (and potentially regulators/auditors) specify and justify a meta-rule describing acceptable model behavior beyond accuracy, including explicit outcomes-based fairness constraints and practical constraints (time, funding, data, talent), and document the reasoning for these criteria.
Failure modes: Governance absent, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Fairness notions: Contestability/recourse, Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Other, Outcome parity
Gap summary (1 sentence): Clarifies that accuracy-maximization leaves substantial freedom among equally accurate models, creating both opportunities (optimize fairness, robustness, recourse) and risks (underspecification, proxy discrimination, loss of justifiability), and proposes meta-rules and aggregation to make selection non-arbitrary, but does not provide concrete operational monitoring or standardized acceptance thresholds for deployment governance.
Measurement procedure: Multiplicity is assessed by comparing models with functionally indistinguishable accuracy and examining differences in decision surfaces (procedural) and prediction disagreement on available labeled data (predictive). The paper relates predictive multiplicity to variance via bias–variance-style decompositions and provides bounds; recommends incorporating explicit constraints (meta-rule) and then using aggregation techniques to select a final predictor from the set of models satisfying the meta-rule.
Metrics used: Conceptual discussion of fairness metrics and disparate impact; uses predictive disagreement between equally accurate models as a key quantity; discusses group-level disparities (selection rates, TPR differences) and individual-level flips; no single standardized fairness metric is mandated.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses legally protected characteristics in regulated domains (e.g., race, sex, age) and proxies (e.g., zip code) especially in lending; fairness concerns framed around demographic groups subject to disparate impact and individuals affected by prediction flips.
QA notes: FAccT’22 paper synthesizing technical, legal, and policy implications of model multiplicity; provides clear definitions and a concrete process recommendation (meta-rule + aggregation) with formal appendix results. Strong on societal/legal framing and trade-offs; empirical validation is mostly by citing prior studies rather than presenting new experiments; operational monitoring/revision governance is discussed at a process level but not specified as lifecycle signals/triggers.
Revision snippet: Implicit governance: recommends an explicit, documented model development and selection process (meta-rule + aggregation) and suggests ongoing consideration of constraints and justifications, but does not specify operational monitoring signals/triggers or formal ownership roles.
SOC snippet: Focuses on normative concerns of arbitrariness and justifiability for individuals subject to adverse outcomes when an equally accurate alternative model could treat them more favorably; links multiplicity to discrimination risk, fairness, recourse, and legal doctrines (e.g., disparate impact) and argues for explicit documentation and policy-minded selection procedures.
Spec snippet: Specifies (i) definitions of multiplicity and measures (pairwise disagreement on available labeled data), (ii) a meta-rule for model acceptability (e.g., minimum accuracy, allowable feature sets, near-equal TPR across groups), and (iii) procedures for resolving residual multiplicity via aggregation (mode predictor, randomized prediction, random model selection) with documentation. Provides formal links between multiplicity, bias–variance, and disagreement in an appendix, but does not prescribe a single universal acceptance threshold.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators, Users
Threshold value: Example meta-rule: accuracy > 95% plus constraints (feature availability/actionability; near-equal TPR across demographic groups). No fixed fairness acceptance threshold required across contexts; thresholds are context-dependent and part of the meta-rule.
Validation snippet: Primarily a conceptual/theoretical and policy analysis paper: synthesizes prior empirical evidence (e.g., equally accurate models differing in fairness/robustness/interpretablity) and provides theoretical results connecting multiplicity to bias–variance; proposes selection/aggregation procedures and legal/policy implications rather than reporting a new deployed-system evaluation.

DES snippet: Demonstrates dataset bias in deception detection by training a classifier to predict sex from features and then using predicted sex as a proxy ‘lie detector’ (predict lie for females, truth for males). Shows this biased proxy achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art deception detectors on biased datasets, and that purported SOTA techniques fail on an unbiased dataset.
ENV snippet: Assumes deception detection is evaluated using supervised ML on small video-based datasets (Real-life Trial: 121 clips; Bag-of-Lies: 325 recordings; Miami University: 320 clips) and that dataset composition and cross-validation design can induce spurious correlations between sensitive attributes (notably biological sex) and the truth/lie label, enabling high reported performance without learning deception.
Failure modes: Governance absent, Other, Unstable subgroup definitions
Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows that headline deception-detection performance can be largely explained by dataset bias (especially sex imbalance) and weak evaluation practices on small datasets, and calls for bias-aware dataset design and sanity checks, but does not provide a deployment-ready fairness requirement with acceptance thresholds or monitoring/revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Train sex classifiers on extracted features (micro-expression vectors, IDT features, gaze features). On test points, predict sex and use it as a proxy for deception (lie if predicted female, truth if predicted male). Compare to classifiers trained normally on truth/lie labels. Use identity-disjoint cross-validation (10-fold for Real-life Trial, 3-fold for Bag-of-Lies) repeated across multiple random fold samplings to quantify variance; evaluate generalization by testing SOTA techniques on a deliberately unbiased dataset (Miami University deception dataset) where all identities have equal truth/lie balance.
Metrics used: Accuracy; AUC; sex-prediction accuracy implicitly; compares biased proxy vs truth/lie-trained classifiers; discusses overestimation from dataset bias and discrimination risk w.r.t. sensitive attributes.
Protected attrs / groups: Biological sex (male/female) is the primary sensitive attribute analyzed; paper notes potential other sensitive attributes (race, age) and other incidental dataset properties (background, clothing, pose) that could be exploited.
QA notes: ICPR 2022 paper auditing deception detection datasets for sex bias: constructs bias-only proxy via sex prediction and shows comparable performance to prior SOTA on biased datasets; evaluates generalization on an unbiased dataset where performance drops to chance. Strong operationalization and offline evaluation; recommendations focus on dataset design/sanity checks rather than lifecycle monitoring/governance.
Revision snippet: Recommends future datasets minimize bias (equal truth/lie per subject, consistent setting per subject) and that researchers perform sensibility checks for dataset bias before claiming high deception-detection performance; does not define an operational post-deployment revision process.
SOC snippet: Highlights ethical risks of AI lie detectors and argues that biased models are discriminatory w.r.t. sensitive attributes (sex, and potentially race/age) and can create harmful false expectations for media/governments; recommends bias-aware dataset construction and sensibility checks before claims of high-stakes capability.
Spec snippet: Operationalizes ‘dataset bias’ by (i) measuring label imbalance across sex in datasets (e.g., females lie more in both Real-life Trial and Bag-of-Lies), and (ii) constructing a bias-only predictor: learn sex from features, then map sex→lie/truth. Evaluates via cross-validation with identity-disjoint folds and reports accuracy/AUC ranges across repeated fold samplings to show variance.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Regulators, Users
Validation snippet: Performs offline experiments on three datasets (Real-life Trial, Bag-of-Lies, Miami University) with identity-disjoint CV; reports that bias-only proxy achieves comparable performance to SOTA on biased datasets and that SOTA techniques drop to chance on the unbiased dataset; includes sensitivity analysis via repeated fold resampling and reports min/mean/max metrics.

DES snippet: Introduces Gopher, a system for fairness debugging that generates compact, interpretable training-data-based explanations of model bias by finding cohesive subsets of the training data that are root causes, using a notion of causal responsibility (bias reduction when removing or updating a subset) and a top-k pattern mining search with pruning for support and diversity.
ENV snippet: Assumes a standard supervised ML pipeline where bias can originate in the training data (measurement errors, misclassifications, imbalance, selection bias, covariate shift, data prep issues, poisoning) and where auditing uses a fairness metric F evaluated on held-out test data (e.g., statistical parity, equal opportunity, predictive parity) w.r.t. a protected attribute S.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a rigorous, testable framework and system (Gopher) for identifying training-data root causes of fairness violations via causal responsibility and pattern mining, but focuses on offline debugging interventions rather than operational monitoring, long-term governance, or stakeholder-driven acceptance thresholds.
Measurement procedure: Compute a fairness metric F on a test set D_test (e.g., difference in positive prediction rate for statistical parity, difference in TPR for equal opportunity, difference in PPV for predictive parity). For a candidate subset S (or pattern-defined subset D(φ)), estimate the change in bias when intervening on training data by deleting or uniformly updating that subset; approximate retraining effects via first/second-order influence functions or one-step gradient descent; rank explanations by interestingness and enforce diversity via containment.
Metrics used: Statistical parity; Equal opportunity; Predictive parity (and notes applicability to other associational and causal notions).
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attribute S (binary in formulation). Experiments use: gender (Adult), age (German), race (SQF).
QA notes: SIGMOD’22 paper introducing Gopher for fairness debugging: patterns over training data subsets + causal responsibility; includes deletion- and update-based explanations with efficient approximations (FO/SO influence, one-step GD) and extensive offline evaluation on benchmark datasets. Strong operationalization and validation; limited stakeholder/elicitation treatment and limited deployment/monitoring governance.
SOC snippet: Frames the motivation as regulatory and social demands for human-understandable explanations and the need to debug discriminatory behavior; discusses fairness as avoiding discrimination w.r.t. protected attributes and motivates actionable interventions on data subsets rather than only reporting bias.
Spec snippet: Defines (i) bias metric F(θ, D_test) where F>0 indicates bias, and uses fairness metrics like statistical parity, equal opportunity, predictive parity; (ii) ‘root cause’ subsets whose removal reduces bias; (iii) causal responsibility RF(S) as relative bias reduction; (iv) pattern interestingness U(φ)=RF(D(φ))/Sup(φ) and diversity via containment threshold to compute top-k explanations.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Bias defined as F(θ,D_test)>0 (unbiased otherwise); uses containment threshold c and support threshold τ for explanation search (not a fixed fairness acceptance threshold).
Validation snippet: Evaluates Gopher end-to-end on standard fairness datasets (German Credit, Adult Income, Stop-Question-Frisk) with logistic regression, SVM, and a feed-forward NN; compares influence approximation accuracy vs retraining and runtime; reports top-k explanations (patterns, support, bias reduction) and update-based explanations; includes scalability analysis.

DES snippet: Defines preference-based user-side fairness via (ε-)envy-freeness and proposes an online certification/auditing procedure (OCEF) that casts envy testing as a pure-exploration multi-armed bandit with conservative exploration constraints, plus an AUDIT wrapper to certify a probabilistic relaxation with guarantees independent of total user count.
ENV snippet: Assumes personalized recommender systems where user preferences are partially observed, disparities in delivered opportunities may be justified by differing preferences, and auditing requires counterfactual exposure (showing user A recommendations intended for user B) plus an assumption of stationarity over the audit horizon (policies and user needs change slowly).
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Proposes a formally specified, preference-based user-side fairness audit for personalized recommenders via ε-envy-freeness and online bandit certification with conservative exploration guarantees, but it assumes stationarity and requires intrusive counterfactual exposure/randomization (raising privacy/ethics and deployment feasibility questions) and does not provide monitoring or revision governance once certified.
Measurement procedure: Model each user’s recommender as a stochastic policy π_m(a|x) with stationary context distribution q_m and reward distribution ν_m; define utility u_m(π) as expected reward. Audit by intermittently replacing a user’s recommendations with those from another user’s policy (counterfactual exposure) to estimate u_m(π_n). Cast each target user’s audit as a bandit with baseline arm 0 (their own policy) and other arms (other users’ policies); run OCEF to decide envy vs ε-no-envy while enforcing conservative constraint on cumulative average performance; run AUDIT by sampling a subset of target users and a subset of arms per target to certify (ε,γ,λ)-envy-freeness with complexity independent of total users M.
Metrics used: Envy metrics: Δ_m = max_n u_m(π_n)−u_m(π_m) (clipped at 0), average envy across users, proportion of ε-envious users; certification parameters ε, α, δ; probabilistic parameters γ, λ; audit evaluation: duration (timesteps to certificate) and cost (baseline utility minus realized utility during audit, can be negative if exploration finds better policies).
Protected attrs / groups: Not centered on protected-attribute groups; focuses on user-side individual preference-based fairness. Mentions sensitive groups as motivation for parity-based audits (e.g., gender disparities in job ads), but the proposed criterion is defined over individuals rather than protected groups.
QA notes: AAAI 2022 paper on auditing personalized recommenders using preference-based fairness (envy-freeness) rather than group parity. Strong formal specification and operationalization through online certification (OCEF) and probabilistic audit (AUDIT) with conservative exploration constraints and duration/cost analysis. Raises ethical/privacy considerations of randomized internal audits but leaves non-stationarity, monitoring, and governance largely to future work.
SOC snippet: Frames recommender systems as shaping information and opportunities (news, jobs, connections) and motivates fairness as preventing unfair delivery of opportunities; discusses ethical/practical issues of randomized audits (intrusiveness, need to follow ethical standards for experiments and check privacy implications of using other users’ policies), but does not provide a full governance process.
Spec snippet: Formalizes ε-envy-freeness for recommender policies π_m: ∀m,n, u_m(π_n) ≤ u_m(π_m)+ε, where u_m(π) is expected utility over contexts and stochastic recommendations. Introduces a probabilistic relaxation (ε,γ,λ)-envy-freeness: for at least (1−λ) fraction of users, the user’s own policy is within the top-γ fraction of utilities over other users’ policies up to ε. Specifies certification outputs (envy vs ε-no-envy) with fixed-confidence guarantees and a conservative exploration constraint requiring anytime average utility during audit to be ≥(1−α) of the baseline system.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Other, Users
Threshold value: Uses explicit tolerance ε in envy definition and certification; uses conservative exploration parameter α; fixed-confidence δ; probabilistic relaxation parameters γ and λ (e.g., experiments set ε=δ=0.05, γ=λ=0.1).
Validation snippet: Provides theoretical guarantees (fixed-confidence correctness, duration/cost bounds under conservative exploration) and evaluates trade-offs via (i) synthetic bandit problems and (ii) simulated recommender tasks on MovieLens-1M and http://Last.fm using matrix completion to generate ground-truth preferences and softmax recommendation policies; compares envy levels, audit duration, and audit cost under different α and settings with/without envy.

DES snippet: Provides a structured review of algorithmic bias in education: defines and situates bias (statistical vs societal), maps bias types to stages/agents in the ML pipeline, surveys formal fairness metrics (independence/separation/sufficiency; similarity-based; causal) and impossibility trade-offs, synthesizes empirical evidence of bias across groups in educational applications, and proposes a framework for moving from unknown bias → known bias → fairness → equity with four mitigation effort areas (improved data collection, tools/resources, openness/incentives, broaden community).
ENV snippet: Assumes educational algorithms are deployed in high-stakes contexts (testing, admissions, dropout prediction, essay scoring, affect detection) where societal and statistical biases can enter throughout the ML lifecycle (task definition → data collection/prep → model development/evaluation → deployment/feedback), and where populations differ across race/ethnicity, gender, nationality and other less-studied categories (SES, disability, military-connected status, etc.).
Elicitation snippet: Synthesizes and categorizes prior empirical studies and fairness definitions to identify which groups are impacted and what pipeline stages/agents contribute to bias; proposes criteria (e.g., non-malleability/immutability of group membership) to prioritize which categories should be studied/collected.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, Other
Fairness notions: Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Synthesizes how algorithmic bias manifests across the educational ML lifecycle and which student groups are impacted, and proposes equity-oriented recommendations (data collection, tooling, incentives, stakeholder inclusion), but remains largely conceptual without concrete acceptance thresholds, operational monitoring, or deployment-ready governance procedures for ongoing bias management.
Measurement procedure: Review paper (not an algorithm) that (i) defines algorithmic bias as unequal model performance across mutually exclusive groups, (ii) maps bias sources (historical, representational, measurement, aggregation, evaluation, deployment) to ML lifecycle stages, (iii) surveys formal fairness metric families and impossibility results, (iv) synthesizes empirical studies showing subgroup performance differences in educational algorithms, and (v) proposes a framework and recommendations to move toward equity (data collection/labeling, tooling, openness/incentives, stakeholder inclusion).
Metrics used: Reviews fairness definitions/metrics: independence (statistical/demographic parity), separation (equal opportunity/equalized odds, predictive parity), sufficiency (calibration), similarity-based fairness, and causal fairness; discusses confusion-matrix based measures (FPR/FNR disparities) and impossibility trade-offs; emphasizes empirical slicing analyses and subgroup performance differences in educational models.
Protected attrs / groups: Race/ethnicity; gender (noting lack of work on non-binary/trans learners); nationality/current national location; socioeconomic status; disability; urbanicity; native language/dialect; parental education background; international student status; military-connected status; plus notes on under-studied groups and intersectionality.
QA notes: IJAIED 2022 survey/review on algorithmic bias in education; strong conceptual framing and mapping of bias sources to ML pipeline stages with extensive synthesis of empirical evidence across many protected and less-studied categories. Provides actionable recommendations (data collection, tools, openness, broaden community) but does not itself operationalize a deployable fairness specification with thresholds or monitoring/revision processes.
SOC snippet: Focuses on concrete harms and affected groups in educational settings, explicitly discussing allocative vs representational harms and which student subpopulations are impacted (race/ethnicity, gender, nationality, SES, disability, urbanicity, parental education, military-connectedness) and calls for equity-oriented practice and broader stakeholder inclusion.
Spec snippet: Proposes a conceptual progression (unknown bias → known bias → fairness → equity) and recommends actions (collect richer demographic data, ensure representativeness/label quality, provide standard bias-metric tooling, create reference datasets, align incentives for disclosure/audits, broaden stakeholder participation). Discusses fairness metric families (independence/separation/sufficiency; similarity-based; causal) and trade-offs/impossibility results, but does not define a single operational acceptance threshold for ‘fair enough’.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Other, Regulators, Users
Validation snippet: Validates claims via narrative synthesis of empirical studies documenting differential performance and error rates across groups in educational applications (e.g., dropout prediction, course failure prediction, essay scoring, speech scoring, affect detectors), plus cross-references to fairness/fairness-metric survey literature and impossibility results; does not report new experimental validation.

DES snippet: Constructs FairFace (108,501 images) with labels for race (7 groups), gender, and age, using controlled collection from YFCC-100M plus other sources and multi-annotator adjudication; trains baseline attribute classifiers (ResNet-34) and provides evaluation protocol for cross-dataset generalization and balanced accuracy; includes benchmarking of commercial APIs.
ENV snippet: Motivates that existing public face datasets are compositionally biased toward White/Caucasian faces, leading to inconsistent accuracy for non-White groups; positions FairFace as a data-centric mitigation by balancing race, gender, and age to improve generalization and enable bias measurement/mitigation in face analytics.
Elicitation snippet: Uses human annotation via Amazon Mechanical Turk with 3 annotators per face (re-annotation if disagreement), plus manual re-verification of items where model predictions disagree with labels; this functions as a structured elicitation/labeling workflow for protected attributes.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Introduces FairFace, a large in-the-wild face attribute dataset balanced across 7 race groups plus gender and age to reduce representation bias and enable more consistent subgroup accuracy and auditing of commercial APIs, but it does not provide deployment-time monitoring/governance for ongoing bias, nor does it define explicit acceptance thresholds for subgroup disparity beyond reporting metrics like ε and STD.
Measurement procedure: Collect images from YFCC-100M (CC BY/SA) with adaptive country sampling to avoid over-dominance by White faces; detect faces (min 50×50) and label race/gender/age via MTurk with agreement rule (2/3 majority; re-annotate otherwise; discard unresolved). Refine labels by training a model and manually re-verifying disagreements. Train ResNet-34 attribute classifiers with Adam; evaluate (i) dataset composition bias via race proportions, (ii) cross-dataset classification across FairFace, UTKFace, LFWA+, CelebA, (iii) generalization to novel datasets (geo-tagged tweets, media photos, protest images) and subgroup consistency (STDV, ε), and (iv) commercial API bias for gender classification.
Metrics used: Balanced accuracy across race/gender/age groups; subgroup-wise accuracies and standard deviations; maximum accuracy disparity ε(Ŷ) based on ratios of conditional accuracies across demographic groups; cross-dataset generalization accuracy for race/gender/age; commercial API accuracy by subgroup; supplemental skin-color (ITA) analyses.
Protected attrs / groups: Race (7 groups: White, Black, Indian, East Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, Latino), gender (M/F), and age groups; also considers skin color (ITA) as a complementary proxy measure but argues race is multidimensional and cannot be reduced to skin tone alone.
QA notes: WACV 2021 paper creating FairFace to address composition bias in face datasets by balancing race (7 groups), gender, and age. Strong dataset-spec + operationalization via subgroup accuracy parity metrics and extensive cross-dataset/generalization evaluations, including audits of commercial APIs. Stakeholder elicitation is limited to labeling workflow (MTurk) rather than participatory governance; acceptance thresholds and monitoring over time are not specified.
Revision snippet: Dataset construction includes iterative label refinement and manual re-verification when model predictions disagree with initial annotations; code/data/models are released (implying ownership for future dataset/model revisions) but no operational monitoring process is described.
SOC snippet: Frames fairness as avoiding demographic performance disparities in face analytics (notably gender classifiers failing on dark-skinned females) and notes downstream harms (public trust, service termination, demographic surveys with policy implications); explicitly treats race, gender, and age as protected attributes and emphasizes representation of groups (Latino, Middle Eastern, SE Asian).
Spec snippet: Defines dataset requirements: balanced composition across 7 race groups (White, Black, Indian, East Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, Latino) with gender and age group labels; defines fairness target as balanced accuracy / accuracy equality across demographic groups (links to equalized-odds/accuracy-equality style constraints). Defines maximum accuracy disparity ε(Ŷ)=max_{j,k∈D} |log(P(Ŷ=Y|A=j)/P(Ŷ=Y|A=k))| as a group-disparity measure; includes protocol for evaluating cross-dataset performance and subgroup gaps.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Regulators, Users
Validation snippet: Validates via extensive offline evaluation: cross-dataset and external generalization tests with subgroup breakdowns, plus an audit-style benchmark of commercial gender classifiers (Amazon, Microsoft, Face++, IBM) on a balanced FairFace subset; reports improved generalization and reduced subgroup disparities for models trained on FairFace.

DES snippet: Proposes AU-Calibrated Facial Expression Recognition (AUC-FER) / AUCFER to mitigate expression annotation bias by using facial action units (AUs) as an auxiliary objective benchmark and adding a triplet-loss regularizer to learn an embedding where samples with similar AUs are close regardless of gender, reducing reliance on biased emotion labels without relabeling.
ENV snippet: Positions supervised CV pipelines as relying on large in-the-wild, crowdsourced datasets where annotations are often assumed unbiased; argues this “gold-standard labels” assumption can be wrong because annotators may share systematic cultural stereotypes, creating label/annotation bias P(Y|X) that propagates to trained models (e.g., facial expression recognition used in video job interviews and other high-impact HCI applications).
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows that facial-expression datasets can encode systematic annotation bias (not just composition bias), which models learn and amplify (notably for happy/angry across gender), and proposes AUC-FER using AU-calibrated triplet loss to mitigate this without relabeling, but it does not address deployment-time monitoring/governance or how to set acceptance thresholds for discrimination in real hiring/emotion-analysis systems.
Measurement procedure: 1) Validate AU recognizer (OpenFace) is not systematically biased across genders using EmotioNet expert-coded AUs; calibrate AU presence thresholds. 2) Quantify annotation bias in expression datasets by comparing P(Happy|AU,gender) (and analogous for Angry) across lab-controlled (KDEF, CFD) vs in-the-wild (ExpW, RAF-DB, AffectNet manual/automatic) and testing independence Y ⫫ Z | AU. 3) Show trained models learn annotation bias: train ResNet-50 on ExpW, test on balanced CFD, compute Disc; compare raw vs AU-conditionally relabeled ExpW. 4) Mitigate without label editing via AUC-FER (triplet loss with AU-based triplets) and compare against debiasing baselines (uniform confusion, gradient projection, domain discriminative/independent).
Metrics used: Annotation-bias tests: conditional happy/angry label proportions by gender given AU6/AU12 (and chi-square / logistic regression significance). Model bias metric: Calders–Verwer discrimination score Disc = P(Ŷ=Happy|F) − P(Ŷ=Happy|M) on balanced test set. Also reports AU detector parity checks (OpenFace AU6/AU12 accuracy/F1 by gender, p-values).
Protected attrs / groups: Gender (M/F) is primary protected attribute; also reports analyses for age and race (noting dataset imbalance limits conclusions).
QA notes: Paper focuses on label/annotation bias P(Y|X) in facial expression recognition, operationalizing fairness as conditional independence of labels from gender given objective AU evidence (AUs as proxy ground truth). Strong empirical evidence across multiple datasets and a mitigation method (AUC-FER) that outperforms composition-bias debiasing baselines. No explicit stakeholder elicitation or governance processes; fairness is evaluated with a discrimination score and conditional tests rather than real-world acceptance criteria.
SOC snippet: Highlights fairness concerns of facial expression recognition and emotion analysis in contexts like algorithmic evaluation of job applicants; links observed label bias to gender stereotypes (women perceived as happier, men as angrier) and argues models trained on biased annotations undermine trust and can discriminate by protected attributes (gender; also analyzes age/race).
Spec snippet: Defines annotation bias as conditional dependence of emotion label Y on protected attribute Z given objective AU evidence: Y ⫫ Z | AU (e.g., P(Y=1|AU6,AU12,Z=M)=P(Y=1|AU6,AU12,Z=F)). Uses AUs (OpenFace) as proxy for objective expression intensity to separate composition bias P(X) from label bias P(Y|X). Proposes AUC-FER loss: L = L_softmax + λ·L_trp, where triplet loss builds triplets (X_i,X_j,X_k) with same AU presence for (i,j) and different AU for k, encouraging AU-consistent embeddings across genders.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Regulators, Users
Threshold value: Hyperparameter λ (triplet-loss weight) and triplet margin α; AU intensity thresholds calibrated to optimize overall AU accuracy on EmotioNet for binarizing AU presence; classification threshold chosen to maximize accuracy on test set.
Validation snippet: Offline experiments across multiple datasets (KDEF, CFD, ExpW, RAF-DB, AffectNet) and architectures (ResNet-50, MobileNetV2). Evaluates fairness via Disc on balanced CFD test set and shows AUC-FER reduces discrimination substantially (e.g., Disc ≈0.006 vs baseline ≈0.059 in one reported setting) while composition-bias mitigation baselines do not fully remove annotation bias.

DES snippet: Provides a theoretical framework connecting construct-based discrimination criteria (disparity amplification, construct accuracy) + worldviews to motivate or critique empirical fairness tests (demographic parity, equalized odds, predictive parity, calibration). Introduces α-disparity test corresponding to α-hybrid worldviews and a stronger disparity-amplification notion using Lipschitz continuity + earthmover distance for numerical constructs.
ENV snippet: Assumes a supervised prediction setting where observed labels may be biased proxies for the (often unobservable) construct; introduces “worldviews” (WAE: construct independent of protected attribute; WYSIWYG: observed labels reflect the construct) and a more realistic α-hybrid worldview to reason about when observed data is biased.
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Fairness notions: Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Other, Outcome parity
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a construct-and-worldview lens for selecting among fairness tests by defining disparity amplification and proving when demographic parity or equalized odds prevent it (under WAE vs WYSIWYG), while showing predictive parity and calibration can still permit amplification, but it remains a theoretical diagnostic framework without empirical case studies or operational monitoring/governance guidance for choosing worldview parameters like α in practice.
Measurement procedure: Mathematical analysis using joint distributions of (Y′,Y,Ŷ,Z) consistent with given worldviews. Proves (a) which empirical tests guarantee no disparity amplification under a worldview and (b) whether they allow construct-optimal models. Provides counterexamples showing predictive parity and calibration can permit or enable disparity amplification (including via threshold post-processing). Proposes α-hybrid worldview to interpolate between WAE and WYSIWYG and derives α-disparity test; extends disparity amplification to numerical constructs using Kantorovich duality and Lipschitz bounds.
Metrics used: Distances and criteria: total variation distance d_tv for output/construct disparity; earthmover distance d_em for numerical constructs; construct accuracy (average Pr[Y′=Ŷ|Z=z] over groups); conditions for fairness tests (DP, EO, predictive parity, calibration); α parameter for α-hybrid worldview and α-disparity test; Lipschitz constant ρ*ℓ for stronger disparity amplification definition.
Protected attrs / groups: Binary protected attribute Z∈{0,1} (e.g., gender, race). Focus is group-level nondiscrimination across protected groups; examples include bail/recidivism and university admissions; links to disparate impact and four-fifths rule context.
QA notes: FAccT 2021 paper that formalizes how different assumptions about bias in observed labels (worldviews) change which empirical fairness test is appropriate if the goal is to avoid disparity amplification relative to an unobservable construct. Strong theoretical contributions (definitions + theorems + counterexamples) and clear implications for why DP vs EO debates persist; however, it does not provide stakeholder elicitation methods for picking the worldview/α parameter, and offers no monitoring or deployment governance guidance.
SOC snippet: Interprets common fairness tests as abstractions of disparate impact (including legal context like the four-fifths rule) and argues that disagreement over fairness tests can stem from different assumptions about data bias (“worldviews”); emphasizes that fairness tests are diagnostics and that no single test/criterion ensures fairness across contexts.
Spec snippet: Defines three spaces (construct Y′, observed Y, prediction Ŷ) and formal empirical tests: demographic parity, equalized odds, predictive parity, calibration. Defines output disparity d_tv(Ŷ|Z=0,Ŷ|Z=1) and disparity amplification when this exceeds construct disparity d_tv(Y′|Z=0,Y′|Z=1). Shows: under WAE, demographic parity prevents disparity amplification and is satisfied by construct-optimal models; under WYSIWYG, equalized odds prevents disparity amplification and is satisfied by construct-optimal models. Proves predictive parity allows arbitrarily large output disparity and calibration is not robust to post-processing (thresholding). Introduces α-hybrid worldview and α-disparity test: d_tv(Ŷ|Z=0,Ŷ|Z=1) ≤ α·d_tv(Y|Z=0,Y|Z=1). Extends disparity amplification for numerical constructs using earthmover distance and Lipschitz constant of ℓ(y′)=Pr[Ŷ=1|Y′=y′].
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: α in the α-hybrid worldview / α-disparity test (α∈[0,1]) parameterizes tolerated output disparity relative to observed disparity. Discusses four-fifths rule / p%-rule as related legal thresholding heuristics (as context), but does not prescribe a numeric α for deployment.
Validation snippet: Validation is primarily theoretical: formal theorems and counterexamples comparing fairness definitions under worldviews; not an empirical evaluation on datasets or deployed systems.

DES snippet: Proposes NIFTY, a unified framework to learn fair and stable GNN representations by (1) adding a Siamese/triplet-style objective that aligns embeddings across noisy and counterfactual graph views, and (2) modifying the GNN architecture with layer-wise Lipschitz (spectral) normalization of weights to improve message passing robustness and promote counterfactual fairness/stability.
ENV snippet: Motivates that GNN representations are used in real-world/high-stakes settings and must be both fair (avoid discriminatory bias tied to sensitive attributes) and stable (robust to small perturbations/attacks on graph structure and node attributes). Introduces application contexts and datasets in criminal justice (bail/recidivism) and financial lending (credit risk/default).
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Fairness notions: Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity
Gap summary (1 sentence): NIFTY unifies counterfactual fairness and stability for GNN representations via augmentation-based objectives and Lipschitz-normalized message passing, demonstrating strong offline improvements on fairness and robustness metrics in high-stakes graph datasets, but it does not specify operational acceptance thresholds or monitoring/revision governance for maintaining these properties after deployment.
Measurement procedure: Generate augmented graph views by (a) masking/noising non-sensitive attributes, (b) flipping binary sensitive attribute to create counterfactual node ũ_s, and (c) dropping edges with probability p_e. Train encoder ENC with Siamese-style objective L_s using cosine distance and stop-gradient; jointly optimize with downstream BCE classification loss L_c using λ. Architecturally, compute per-layer Lipschitz constants via spectral norm and normalize weights W_a^k to bound embedding sensitivity; theoretically derive bounds (product of layer norms) for stability and counterfactual unfairness.
Metrics used: Counterfactual unfairness rate (percent of test nodes whose predicted label changes when sensitive attribute is flipped); instability rate (percent whose prediction changes under random attribute noise/edge perturbations); group fairness metrics ΔSP (statistical parity difference) and ΔEO (equal opportunity difference); predictive performance AUROC and F1-score.
Protected attrs / groups: Binary sensitive attribute s per dataset: gender (German credit), race (recidivism/bail), age (credit defaulter). Counterfactual fairness is operationalized by flipping s; group fairness measured via ΔSP and ΔEO between s=0 vs s=1 groups.
QA notes: UAI 2021 paper proposing NIFTY to jointly enforce counterfactual fairness and stability in GNN embeddings. Clear formal definitions, objective (λ-weighted task + similarity loss), architectural Lipschitz/spectral normalization, theoretical bounds, and extensive offline evaluation/ablation on lending and criminal-justice graphs. No stakeholder elicitation process; governance/monitoring over time not covered; fairness acceptance criteria/thresholds not specified beyond tunable λ and perturbation parameters.
SOC snippet: Frames fairness as avoiding discriminatory bias and specifically targets invariance to sensitive attributes (e.g., gender, race, age) in high-stakes decisions. Discusses fairness notions (group, individual, counterfactual) and evaluates both counterfactual unfairness and group fairness gaps (ΔSP, ΔEO) on datasets with protected attributes.
Spec snippet: Defines requirements for the encoder ENC: counterfactual fairness ENC(u)=ENC(ũ_s) when flipping sensitive attribute s; stability via Lipschitz continuity |ENC(ũ)−ENC(u)|_p ≤ L|b̃_u−b_u|_p under attribute/edge perturbations. Specifies NIFTY training objective min[(1−λ)L_c + λ L_s], where L_s is a (stop-grad) similarity loss aligning embeddings of original nodes with their perturbed and counterfactual counterparts, and adds layer-wise weight normalization using Lipschitz/spectral norms to bound representation changes; provides theorems bounding stability and counterfactual unfairness.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other
Threshold value: Regularization coefficient λ controls trade-off between task loss and fairness/stability objective (used λ=0.6; sensitivity over λ). Perturbation probabilities p_n (attribute) and p_e (edge drop) are parameters; no fixed acceptance thresholds for fairness metrics are specified.
Validation snippet: Evaluates NIFTY on three constructed graph datasets (German credit, Recidivism/bail, Credit defaulter) across five GNN backbones (GCN, GraphSAGE, JK, GIN, InfoMax). Reports AUROC/F1 plus unfairness/instability rates and ΔSP/ΔEO; includes ablation (objective vs architecture) and sensitivity to λ; compares against FairGCN and RobustGCN baselines.

DES snippet: Proposes MIAP: an updated annotation protocol producing exhaustive person bounding boxes on a 100k-image subset, plus attribute labels for perceived gender presentation and perceived age range, specifically to enable fairness analysis and study effects of partial annotations.
ENV snippet: Highlights that large CV datasets use partial, non-exhaustive annotations; warns that systematic missing person boxes can yield misleading fairness evaluation and biased detectors, especially when subgroup labels (man/woman/boy/girl) interact with the annotation pipeline and societal norms.
Elicitation snippet: Annotation guidelines remind annotators that gender-expression norms vary across cultures/time and instruct them to avoid rigid stereotype-based rules; includes explicit instructions and examples to mitigate unconscious bias and to use “unknown” when uncertain.
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, Other, Unstable subgroup definitions
Gap summary (1 sentence): Introduces MIAP, an exhaustive person-annotation subset of Open Images with perceived gender-presentation and age-range labels designed for fairness research, showing how partial annotation pipelines can encode societal norms and create systematic missing ground truth, but it does not provide deployment-time monitoring/revision governance and explicitly restricts use of these labels to fairness research rather than building gender/age classifiers.
Measurement procedure: Analyze Open Images annotation pipeline (image-level labels proposed by classifiers + human verification → boxes for most specific positive labels) and show how person subclasses cause systematic omissions when collapsing to person. Create MIAP by relabeling 100k images (70k train, 30k val/test) with at least one person-subtree positive label: draw exhaustive person boxes; label each box with perceived gender presentation and perceived age range; compare original vs MIAP box counts per split/attribute; compute nPMI(Missing,label)−nPMI(Not Missing,label) to identify attributes correlated with missing boxes.
Metrics used: Dataset bias/fairness support metrics: counts of missing vs added person boxes; subgroup distribution comparisons; normalized pointwise mutual information (nPMI) to measure association between missing boxes and attribute labels; motivates downstream fairness evaluation via disaggregated metrics across gender-presentation and age-range slices (e.g., equality of opportunity) but does not itself define model-level acceptance thresholds.
Notes: Set Thresholds stated = No and Acceptance criteria = No because the paper focuses on dataset annotation and bias analysis rather than specifying acceptance thresholds for model fairness.
Protected attrs / groups: Perceived gender presentation (predominantly feminine, predominantly masculine, unknown) and perceived age range (young, middle, older, unknown) for each person box; explicitly distinguishes presentation from identity/actual age. Notes skin tone (e.g., Fitzpatrick type) as important but not covered.
QA notes: AIES’21 poster paper introducing MIAP (More Inclusive Annotations for People), a 100k-image relabeling of Open Images with exhaustive person boxes plus attributes for perceived gender presentation and perceived age range. Key fairness contribution is showing how the original person-subtree labeling and partial-annotation pipeline creates systematic missing boxes correlated with gendered/age-related labels and image context, which can distort both training and fairness evaluation. Provides statistical analysis (nPMI), count comparisons, and explicit intended-use/ethics caveats (do not build gender/age classifiers). Limitations include remaining imbalance (predominantly masculine), no skin tone attribute, and partial coverage of other taxonomy elements (relationships/triplets not re-annotated).
SOC snippet: Explicitly discusses how annotation procedures interact with societal norms and language (e.g., gender stereotypes, ‘girl’ vs ‘boy’) and cautions against harmful uses of gender/age labeling; recommends using labels for fairness evaluation only and not for deploying gender/age classifiers.
Spec snippet: Defines a relabeling protocol: select images with any positive person-subtree label, draw boxes around all visible people, and assign attributes for perceived gender presentation (predominantly feminine/masculine/unknown) and perceived age range (young/middle/older/unknown); provides quantitative comparisons and statistics (e.g., nPMI) to analyze missing-box patterns.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other
Validation snippet: Validates the annotation changes via comparative analysis on 100k-image subset: reports added-box rate (~30% images with additional boxes), box-count deltas by attribute and split, and statistical association (nPMI) between missing boxes and attribute labels; provides examples/visualizations of new boxes.

DES snippet: Provides a structured taxonomy of choices/assumptions in prediction-based decisions and a notationally consistent catalog of formal fairness definitions (oblivious parity metrics, metric/individual fairness, and causal/path-specific notions), plus impossibility results showing incompatibilities among definitions.
ENV snippet: Frames fairness issues in prediction-based decision-making deployed in real domains (lending, pretrial detention, welfare, hiring) and highlights key background assumptions (population selection, decision space, measurement) that shape fairness outcomes beyond the model itself.
Elicitation snippet: Argues for involving impacted communities across the development process and calls for community input, independent revalidation, and meaningful oversight (example: pretrial risk assessment statement of civil rights concerns).
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, Other
Fairness notions: Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Offers a comprehensive, consistent catalog of fairness definitions and the often-implicit assumptions behind prediction-based decision-making (including data/measurement and policy framing), highlighting impossibility trade-offs and urging documentation and community oversight, but does not provide an end-to-end operational method for turning these considerations into concrete, monitored fairness requirements for a deployed system.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual synthesis: lays out decision-theoretic setup (V=(A,X), score S=ψ(V), decision D=δ(V)), links fairness notions to conditional independence statements and confusion-matrix rates; explains impossibility results (e.g., separation vs sufficiency under unequal base rates and imperfect prediction) and when different fairness notions are motivated (measurement error, visibility of decisions, stakeholder perspectives).
Metrics used: Catalogs parity-based metrics from the confusion matrix (FPR, TPR, FNR, TNR, PPV, NPV, accuracy), equalized odds/separation, sufficiency/predictive parity/calibration, demographic parity/statistical parity, metric fairness/individual fairness (Lipschitz), and causal counterfactual/path-specific definitions (counterfactual fairness, no direct effect, path-specific effects). Also discusses assumptions like selective labels, measurement error (including differential measurement error), and dynamic/temporal assumptions (no interference, static vs dynamic evaluation).
Monitoring signals: Calls for regular revalidation, independent audits, and documentation of data/model performance (e.g., model cards, datasheets) plus public reporting and oversight.
Monitoring snippet: Recommends documentation and oversight, and notes a principle that tools should be “revalidated regularly by independent data scientists” with community input and subjected to oversight.
Notes: Set Monitoring/Revision to YES because the paper explicitly recommends regular revalidation/auditing and oversight, though not as a detailed operational process.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses protected characteristics (race, gender, class) and intersectionality (e.g., Black women; darker-skinned females) and notes cultural/context dependence of salient group categories; emphasizes that entry into the modeled population (e.g., arrest, loan application) can itself be biased.
QA notes: Annual Review (2021) that systematizes algorithmic fairness for prediction-based decision-making: clarifies policy/problem-formulation assumptions (goals, population selection, decision space), data issues (selective labels, sampling bias, differential measurement error, societal bias), and provides a notationally consistent catalog of fairness definitions (parity metrics from confusion matrix, calibration/sufficiency, equalized odds/separation, demographic parity, metric fairness, and causal/path-specific notions). Emphasizes impossibility results, documentation/auditing, and community input/oversight; limited on concrete RE-style lifecycle artifacts or implementable acceptance/monitoring triggers beyond high-level recommendations.
Revision snippet: Suggests regular revalidation and meaningful oversight as part of responsible deployment; does not specify operational triggers/owners in a concrete system lifecycle.
SOC snippet: Explicitly connects formal fairness metrics to social goals, stakeholders, and power structures (e.g., policing and lending institutions), emphasizes community input/oversight and documentation, and warns about gaps between mathematical convenience and societal objectives.
Spec snippet: Defines many fairness criteria as parity/conditional independence constraints over (A,X,Y,S,D), e.g., equalized odds (D ⟂ A | Y), sufficiency (Y ⟂ A | D or S), demographic parity (D ⟂ A), and individual/metric fairness (|δ(v)−δ(v′)| ≤ m(v,v′)).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Discusses threshold-based decision rules δ(v)=I(ψ(v)≥c) and notes thresholding choices; does not prescribe universal numeric thresholds (context-dependent).
Validation snippet: Review article that surveys and harmonizes definitions/assumptions/terminology, using canonical examples (pretrial risk assessment and lending) to illustrate how fairness criteria, data issues, and decision assumptions interact; not an empirical evaluation of a new algorithm.

DES snippet: Introduces two algorithmic frameworks for best-effort group fairness guarantees in classification: (1) Proportional Fairness (PF) classifier when considering all possible groups, and (2) BeFair (Best-effort Fair) approach for more structured group classes (e.g., linearly separable groups), using a minmax/fictitious-play style procedure and convex relaxations.
ENV snippet: Frames deployment contexts (criminal justice, credit/lending, healthcare) as high-stakes and motivates fairness due to disparate impact/bias; argues that standard fairness depends on pre-specified protected groups which may be missing, unusable, or mis-specified.
Failure modes: No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other, Unstable subgroup definitions
Fairness notions: Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity
Gap summary (1 sentence): Proposes best-effort group fairness guarantees that compare each group’s performance to the best achievable on that group (PF for all subgroups, BeFair for structured subgroup classes), showing empirically improved worst-group fidelity versus ERM, but it remains largely an offline optimization/evaluation approach without operational monitoring or governance processes for maintaining guarantees over time.
Measurement procedure: Formal model: define groups g u2286 N, u_g(h) as mean correctness over g, and h*g maximizing u_g over H. For PF: optimize u2211_i ln u_i(h) over randomized classifiers u0394(H) and prove worst-case subgroup guarantees. For BeFair: define err_g(h) and solve min_h err_N(h) subject to best-effort constraints for all g in a structured group class; recast into min-max game, use fictitious play, with learner best response as weighted ERM and adversary best response approximated via convex relaxation; evaluate on Adult and COMPAS datasets using overall accuracy and MAEu03b4.
Metrics used: Group-wise accuracy / error rate (err_g); PF utility u_g(h) and individual utilities u_i(h); MAE_u03b4(h)=max_g err_g(h) u2212 u03b4·err_g(h*g) as evaluation metric for best-effort fidelity; reports overall test accuracy and MAEu03b4 curves for Adult and COMPAS benchmarks.
Protected attrs / groups: Motivates protected groups like race and gender but key contribution is handling unknown/unobserved/mis-specified groups. Considers group class G as (a) all subsets of the data (2^N) and (b) structured groups such as linearly separable groups; experiments use Adult/COMPAS (which include sensitive attributes like race/sex, but the framework is not limited to a fixed list).
QA notes: AISTATS 2021 paper defining “best-effort” fairness: guarantee each group g achieves performance close to its own optimum (h*g), addressing harms of absolute parity-style constraints and the problem of unknown/mis-specified groups. Provides PF theoretical guarantees for all subgroups (2^N) and BeFair algorithm for linearly separable groups via min-max formulation, fictitious play, and convex relaxation; evaluates on Adult and COMPAS using MAEu03b4. No stakeholder elicitation methods; governance/monitoring are not treated beyond offline evaluation.
SOC snippet: Discusses societal risks and unintended consequences of group mis-specification and reliance on sensitive attributes (legal constraints, treatment parity, heterogeneous populations, resentment), and uses fairness notions (parity/equalized odds) as potentially harmful when groups differ in intrinsic difficulty; positions best-effort guarantees as reducing harm to easier-to-classify groups while still protecting harder groups.
Spec snippet: Defines a best-effort fairness constraint relative to each group’s optimal achievable performance: for all groups g in a class G, guarantee performance of h on g compared with h_g. For G=2^N, PF maximizes u2211_i ln u_i(h) and guarantees u_g(hPF) u2265 |g|/|N| for any perfectly-classifiable subgroup. For structured G (linearly separable groups), formulates BeFair(u03b3): minimize overall error s.t. u2200g, err_g(h) u2264 err_g(h_g)+u03b3 (or u03b4·err_g(h*_g)+u03b3), solved via min-max game with learner/adversary best responses and convexification of the adversary subproblem.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: BeFair uses additive u03b3 and multiplicative u03b4 parameters; experiments sweep u03b4 (e.g., 1.0 to 1.30) and report MAE_u03b4, with example results that u03b4u22481.10 yields MAE u22481% on benchmarks (as reported).
Validation snippet: Empirical evaluation on Adult (UCI) and COMPAS datasets: compares ERM baselines (logistic regression, AdaBoost) vs PF heuristic and u03b4-BeFair; reports overall test accuracy and computes MAE_u03b4(h) over linearly separable groups for varying u03b4, showing substantial improvements of BeFair over ERM and low MAE for small u03b4>1.

Citation: Prince, Anya E.R., and Daniel Schwarcz. "Proxy Discrimination in the Age of Artificial Intelligence and Big Data." Iowa Law Review 105 (2020): 1257–1318. https://scholarship.law.umn.edu/faculty_articles/682
DES snippet: Defines proxy discrimination as a subset of disparate impact where the usefulness of a facially neutral practice derives (at least in part) from the disparate impact itself; argues AI + big data make unintentional but rational proxy discrimination more likely by discovering less intuitive proxies when direct protected-class data are excluded.
ENV snippet: Frames the problem as a fundamental challenge to antidiscrimination regimes in real-world socio-technical domains (insurance, employment, criminal justice) due to expanded data collection and opacity; emphasizes regulators’/firms’ limited ability to understand model behavior and the legal structure of ‘directly predictive’ protected traits.
Failure modes: Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Clarifies proxy discrimination as distinct from general disparate impact and argues AI systems will inevitably seek proxies for directly predictive protected traits, motivating policy/technical strategies (data collection, transparency, approved-factor rules, and de-proxying models) but without specifying implementable lifecycle monitoring/revision mechanisms.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual legal analysis with illustrative hypotheticals and econometric intuition (e.g., controlling for protected traits to test whether a variable’s predictive power derives from proxying).
Metrics used: Not a technical metrics paper; discusses disparate impact generally and uses conceptual/econometric framing (omitted variable bias) to explain proxies.
Notes: Use for RQ1/RQ3: provides strong conceptual foundations on proxies, ‘directly predictive’ protected traits, and why removing protected attributes is insufficient; also provides a policy strategy menu (approved factors, protected-class data collection/disclosure, de-proxying statistical models).
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses legally protected classes broadly (race, sex, disability, genetics, preexisting conditions) and how algorithms can reconstruct or proxy for protected attributes via correlated variables (e.g., ZIP code, behavioral data).
QA notes: Conceptual legal analysis (not an empirical/technical system paper). Strong on objectives, detailed argument structure, and practical policy relevance; weak on technical operationalization and empirical validation because it does not propose or test concrete metrics/thresholds or implementations. Discusses ethical/legitimacy implications and policy strategies, but provides no lifecycle monitoring/revision procedure or acceptance criteria.
SOC snippet: Analyzes normative harms: proxy discrimination undermines goals of antidiscrimination regimes that aim to prevent outcome disparities for protected groups, even when discrimination is ‘rational’; discusses societal goals such as risk sharing, anti-stereotyping, preventing chilling effects, and reversing past discrimination.
Spec snippet: Introduces the key condition for proxy discrimination: a facially neutral practice producing disparate impact constitutes proxy discrimination only when the practice’s usefulness derives in part from producing that disparate impact. Distinguishes intentional vs unintentional proxy discrimination and direct (causal/opaque) vs indirect proxy discrimination pathways in AI contexts.

Citation: Sharma, Arnab, and Heike Wehrheim. “Automatic Fairness Testing of Machine Learning Models.” In Testing Software and Systems (ICTSS 2020), 32nd IFIP International Conference on Testing Software and Systems, Naples, Italy, Dec 2020, pp. 255–271. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-64881-7_16. HAL: hal-03239825 (submitted 27 May 2021): https://inria.hal.science/hal-03239825v1
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Introduces a formal, testable fairness-specification language and a verification-based testing (SMT/Z3) method that finds discriminatory counterexample pairs more effectively than prior black-box fairness testers on standard tabular benchmarks, but does not provide deployment-time monitoring/revision or clear acceptance criteria for declaring a model sufficiently fair.
Measurement procedure: Verification-based testing (VBT): infer a white-box decision tree approximation of the black-box model using oracle data from model predictions; translate decision tree + fairness assume/assert spec to SMT (Z3) to search for counterexample pairs (x,y) that satisfy assumptions and violate fairness; validate candidates on the black-box model; use pruning (branch + data-instance) to enumerate multiple counterexamples; retrain decision tree to improve approximation when candidates are invalid.
Metrics used: Similarity-based fairness (metamorphic relation): individual discrimination; group discrimination; fairness through unawareness; fairness through awareness (distance metric d with threshold ε). Effectiveness measured by number of discriminatory (valid unfair) test cases found under a test budget; also reports detection rate (#valid/#total candidate cases).
Protected attrs / groups: Benchmarks include Adult (gender, race) and German Credit (gender, age) as protected/sensitive features; paper discusses protected/sensitive attributes generally (e.g., gender, age).
QA notes: Technical testing paper. Strong: clear objective (fairness as a requirement; automatic fairness testing), detailed approach (spec language + VBT workflow with decision-tree approximation, SMT encoding in Z3, pruning strategies), and empirical evaluation vs AEQUITAS and SG on Adult/German datasets. Partial/weak: acceptance criteria for when fairness is 'enough' is budget-based; limited discussion of deployment governance (monitoring/revision), and stakeholder/elicitation aspects are not addressed beyond motivating fairness as a requirement.
Spec snippet: “Our idea is to develop a property-based testing mechanism for fairness checking where the specific fairness requirement can be specified using an assume-assert construct.”
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Users
Threshold value: Fairness through awareness uses a user-chosen distance threshold ε (example ε=0.1). No fixed global acceptance threshold for declaring a model 'fair' beyond finding counterexamples within a budget.
Validation snippet: “The empirical evaluation on several benchmark ML models shows verification-based testing to perform better than existing fairness testing techniques with respect to effectiveness.”

Citation: Chakraborty, Joymallya, Suvodeep Majumder, Zhe Yu, and Tim Menzies. "Fairway: A Way to Build Fair ML Software." In Proceedings of the 28th ACM Joint European Software Engineering Conference and Symposium on the Foundations of Software Engineering (ESEC/FSE '20), 654–665. Virtual Event, USA, Nov 8–13, 2020. ACM. https://doi.org/10.1145/3368089.3409697
DES snippet: Proposes Fairway, a combined pipeline: (1) pre-processing to detect and remove/clean ambiguous (potentially biased) training points via training separate models on privileged vs unprivileged groups and removing points where predictions disagree, then (2) in-processing via multi-objective hyperparameter optimization (FAIR_FLASH, FLASH-based SMBO) to reduce fairness metrics while preserving predictive performance (recall/false alarm).
