ENV snippet: Positions fairness as a critical non-functional requirement for ML software used in high-stakes domains (finance, hiring, admissions, criminal justice, healthcare) and argues bias can be injected across the ML/SE lifecycle; proposes that bias testing and mitigation should be routine parts of ML software development.
Elicitation snippet: No explicit stakeholder elicitation study; motivation includes a claim that fairness testing/mitigation should be routine in the ML software development lifecycle and highlights practitioner usability concerns (comprehensible method).
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a concrete, implementable pipeline for bias detection (training-data ambiguity via group-conditioned models + situation testing) and mitigation (multi-objective hyperparameter optimization) with explicit group-fairness metrics, but does not specify deployment-time monitoring, drift handling, or clear acceptance thresholds/decision rules for when a model is “fair enough.”
Measurement procedure: Train baseline logistic regression (scikit-learn defaults) and measure recall/FAR and fairness metrics (AOD/EOD). Pre-processing: split data by protected attribute into privileged vs unprivileged groups, train separate models, flag “ambiguous” training points where group-conditioned models disagree, drop them (or suggest relabeling as future work). In-processing: tune model hyperparameters with FAIR_FLASH (CART surrogate model + acquisition) to optimize multiple objectives (higher recall, lower FAR, lower AOD, lower EOD) on validation set; report medians over 10 random shuffles.
Metrics used: Equal Opportunity Difference (EOD) and Average Odds Difference (AOD) (absolute values) computed from confusion matrices for privileged vs unprivileged groups; predictive performance metrics: recall and false alarm rate; additional bias indicator via situation testing (% points where prediction flips when protected attribute is toggled).
Notes: Strong SE-oriented framing: fairness as NFR and lifecycle activity; operationalization is group-metric based + data cleaning + hyperparameter optimization. Does not cover post-deployment monitoring or revision triggers/ownership.
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attributes include sex, race, age (dataset-dependent). Privileged/unprivileged group definitions given per dataset (e.g., Adult: male vs female; white vs non-white; COMPAS: caucasian vs not caucasian; etc.).
QA notes: Clear SE motivation and detailed method (pre-process ambiguous points + FAIR_FLASH multi-objective tuning) with explicit metrics (AOD/EOD, recall/FAR) and repeated experiments across 5 datasets; includes threats-to-validity and notes metric shortcomings (class imbalance, sampling, cost). Limited on stakeholder elicitation and on lifecycle governance beyond offline evaluation (no monitoring/revision plan or acceptance thresholds).
SOC snippet: Frames harm as “algorithmic discrimination” that advantages privileged groups (sex/race/etc.) and disadvantages unprivileged groups; uses real examples (e.g., facial recognition, recruiting) to motivate; treats fairness as a socio-technical quality attribute and emphasizes comprehensibility for practitioners.
Spec snippet: Defines fairness for binary classification via group fairness goals and operationalizes with metrics EOD (TPR_U − TPR_P) and AOD = 0.5*[(FPR_U − FPR_P)+(TPR_U − TPR_P)], using absolute values; also uses situation testing (change protected attribute and check prediction change) as a bias indicator.
Threshold value: Uses train/validation/test split (70/15/15), repeated experiments (10 shuffles). Uses FLASH stopping criterion via “life” parameter; does not define a domain acceptance threshold for AOD/EOD (treated as lower is better).
Validation snippet: Evaluates on five UCI datasets (Adult, COMPAS, German Credit, Default Credit, Heart Health) using logistic regression; compares baseline vs pre-processing vs optimization vs Fairway and against selected AIF360 baselines; reports improvements in AOD/EOD with limited performance degradation and reduced situation-testing failures.

Citation: Hellman, Deborah. "Measuring Algorithmic Fairness." Virginia Law Review 106, no. 4 (June 2020): 811–866. https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.2307/27074708
DES snippet: Distinguishes families of fairness measures (predictive parity / calibration vs error-rate parity) and argues they target different tasks (belief vs action). Proposes prioritizing error-ratio parity as an indicator of potential unfairness, and argues overall accuracy improvements can reduce unfairness; also argues legal doctrine is not categorically opposed to using protected traits within algorithms to improve accuracy/fairness.
ENV snippet: Frames algorithmic decision-making as increasingly common and controversial due to concerns about transparency, accountability, and fairness; uses COMPAS / ProPublica vs Northpointe debate to motivate why measuring fairness is fraught and why base rates + measurement error matter.
Failure modes: Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a clear conceptual, normative, and legal argument for prioritizing error-ratio concerns over calibration as a fairness signal and for permissibility of using protected traits to improve accuracy, but does not provide operational RE artifacts (elicitation, testable acceptance criteria, monitoring signals, or revision governance) to turn these fairness ideas into lifecycle requirements.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual/normative legal-philosophical analysis using confusion matrices to define PPV/NPV and error rates; analyzes trade-offs under different base rates and discusses implications of measurement error (e.g., arrests as proxy for crime) and compounding injustice.
Metrics used: Discusses fairness measures rather than proposing a single metric to implement: calibration/predictive parity (PPV/NPV meaning of scores across groups) vs false positive/false negative rates and their ratio (error ratio parity).
Protected attrs / groups: Focuses on legally protected groups (especially race; also sex) in algorithmic scoring contexts; uses black/white examples and discusses protected classifications in anti-discrimination law.
QA notes: Strong conceptual + normative framing of fairness measurement (calibration vs error-rate parity) with clear worked examples (confusion matrices) and explicit discussion of measurement error and compounding injustice. It does not provide an empirical evaluation of an algorithm or propose a concrete, testable requirements/monitoring framework; operational guidance is mainly argumentative and legal-doctrinal (when use of protected traits may be permissible) rather than implementable acceptance criteria.
SOC snippet: Argues disparities in error ratios matter because they can indicate biased measurement (e.g., arrests vs true offending) or compounding prior injustice, raising moral reasons to scrutinize data and to hesitate in deploying tools that entrench prior inequities.
Spec snippet: Asks which fairness measure to prioritize when predictive parity (calibration) and error-rate parity cannot both be satisfied under differing base rates; argues predictive parity is about what to believe, while error ratios relate to decision/action and can signal unfairness.

Citation: Kawamoto, Yusuke. "Towards Logical Specification of Statistical Machine Learning." arXiv:1907.10327v2 [cs.LO] (28 Mar 2020).
DES snippet: Proposes a distributional Kripke model + statistical epistemic logic (StatEL) to formalize statistical classification properties. Encodes performance (confusion-matrix metrics), robustness (via divergence-based accessibility relations between perturbed-input worlds), and fairness (via conditional indistinguishability) in logical formulas; introduces counterfactual epistemic operators to express fairness notions.
ENV snippet: Motivates a gap: while robustness verification/testing for ML (especially DNNs) has advanced, formal specification of ML properties is relatively underdeveloped; positions logical approaches as useful for classifying/relating desired security and ML properties (performance, robustness, fairness).
Failure modes: Governance absent, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Introduces a rigorous logical specification framework (StatEL + distributional Kripke models + counterfactual epistemic operators) for performance, robustness, and fairness properties of classifiers, but does not provide empirical validation or scalable checking/model-checking procedures nor any deployment-time monitoring/revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Defines a distributional Kripke model where each possible world is a probability distribution over states; assigns measurement variables (input x, true label y, predicted label ŷ) with stochastic semantics. Uses divergence/metric-based accessibility relations (e.g., total variation on outputs, Wasserstein on inputs) to interpret epistemic operators. Formalizes performance via conditional probabilities (e.g., Precision: ψ_ℓ(x) ⊃ P_I h_ℓ(x)); robustness via K^D_ε over perturbed-input worlds; fairness via counterfactual operators expressing conditional indistinguishability.
Metrics used: Formal logical counterparts of confusion-matrix metrics (precision/recall/accuracy, FPR/FNR etc.) and fairness notions (statistical parity/group fairness, individual fairness as Lipschitz continuity, equal opportunity) expressed as StatEL formulas; robustness expressed via divergence/metric constraints between input distributions (e.g., Wasserstein, total variation) and knowledge operators.
Notes: Primarily a specification language / formalization contribution. Explicitly notes it does not provide checking/guaranteeing/improving methods; leaves testing/model checking for future work.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses protected attributes and groups abstractly; uses group membership predicates η_G(x) and compares groups G0 vs G1 in statistical parity and equal opportunity formulas; does not fix a domain-specific protected attribute list in the formalism.
QA notes: Strong formal/specification paper: clear logical language (StatEL) and semantics, with explicit formalizations of common performance metrics, robustness notions, and several fairness definitions using counterfactual epistemic operators. It explicitly states it does not provide checking/guarantees/improvement or empirical evaluation; limitations include lack of scalable model checking/testing algorithms and limited coverage of system/process-level aspects (monitoring, revision, data/learning pipeline issues).
SOC snippet: Frames robustness and fairness of ML as safety/security concerns in real-world systems; fairness is treated as indistinguishability/impact parity across groups/individuals and formalized using counterfactual knowledge operators (conceptual contribution rather than empirical study).
Spec snippet: Defines StatEL syntax/semantics (probability quantification, conditional probability, epistemic operators over divergence-based accessibility) and uses it to formally define metrics like precision/recall/accuracy, robustness properties (e.g., K^D_ε Recall), and fairness properties (statistical parity, individual fairness as Lipschitz, equal opportunity) as logical formulas.
Threshold value: Abstract thresholds/parameters: ε for divergences/metrics defining accessibility; δ for robustness probability bounds; intervals I for probability constraints (e.g., P_(0.95,1]); significance level α and sample size n appear in the StatEL background (statistical secrecy) but the ML-spec paper is primarily about specification rather than selecting numeric thresholds.

Citation: Bietti, Elettra. "From Ethics Washing to Ethics Bashing: A View on Tech Ethics from Within Moral Philosophy." In Proceedings of the 2020 Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAT\ '20)*, 210–219. Barcelona, Spain, Jan 27–30, 2020. ACM. https://doi.org/10.1145/3351095.3372860
DES snippet: Conceptualizes and critiques two patterns: “ethics washing” (industry instrumentalization of ethics language/structures to justify self-regulation) and “ethics bashing” (trivializing ethics/moral philosophy by equating it with corporate ethics boards or dismissing it as mere PR/ivory-tower). Proposes ethics as a mode of inquiry to evaluate competing tech policy strategies.
ENV snippet: Frames “ethics” rhetoric in tech policy as contested and often weaponized to support deregulation/self-regulation; situates discussion in real governance episodes (e.g., Google ATEAC) and argues we need to view technology ethics from within moral philosophy rather than only as corporate governance structures.
Failure modes: Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a strong conceptual and normative governance critique of tech-ethics rhetoric (ethics washing vs ethics bashing) and defends moral philosophy as inquiry, but offers no operational RE artifacts (elicitation outputs, measurable acceptance criteria, monitoring signals, or revision ownership) for implementing governance over time.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual analysis from moral philosophy: defines ethics washing and ethics bashing; analyzes limitations/roles of moral philosophy (instrumental vs intrinsic value), and discusses governance implications via illustrative cases and argumentation (no empirical measurement).
Metrics used: Not applicable (conceptual/philosophical governance critique; no technical fairness metrics).
Notes: Primarily useful for RQ1/RQ3 at the governance/ethics layer: clarifies why ethics codes/boards can be performative and how critiques can accidentally trivialize moral inquiry; supports framing governance requirements beyond ‘principles lists’.
Protected attrs / groups: Not focused on protected attributes directly; discusses impacts on marginalized stakeholders and references anti-LGBT backlash case (ATEAC), and broader social justice concerns in tech governance.
QA notes: Conceptual/philosophical paper with clear definitions and structured argument; strong relevance for governance and ethics-inquiry framing. No empirical validation, no technical operationalization, and no lifecycle monitoring/revision process (ownership/triggers/acceptance criteria) beyond critique and recommendations.
SOC snippet: Argues that ethics washing can shift burdens to marginalized stakeholders and narrow public imagination; stresses the role of moral philosophy in dialogue, principled reasoning, and scrutinizing power/inequality within technology governance debates.
Spec snippet: Normative claim: tech policy should move beyond both “ethics washing” and “ethics bashing” by treating ethics as an intrinsically valuable mode of inquiry that can assess governance strategies, rather than reducing ethics to boards/codes or to corporate cover.

DES snippet: “FlipTest leverages optimal transport to match individuals in different protected groups, creating similar pairs of in-distribution samples.”
ENV snippet: “We present FlipTest, a black-box technique for uncovering discrimination in classifiers… had an individual been of a different protected status, would the model have treated them differently?”
Failure modes: Governance absent, No thresholds / acceptance criteria
Gap summary (1 sentence): Strong black-box individual/subgroup discrimination testing and diagnostic reporting, but does not define deployment acceptance thresholds, monitoring signals, or a revision/ownership process.
Measurement procedure: Construct optimal transport map G between protected groups (uses GAN-approximated OT with cost = squared L1; generator loss adds transport cost term). For each x in source group S, compare h(x) vs h(G(x)) to form flipsets; compare flipset distributions vs population to identify affected subgroups; compute transparency report rankings from feature differences between x and G(x).
Metrics used: Flipset size and asymmetry: |F+(h,G)| and |F−(h,G)|; net flipset size and relation to demographic parity / equalized odds; transparency report feature rankings based on average (x−G(x)) and sign(x−G(x)) over flipset.
Notes: Uses optimal transport between protected-group distributions; OT approximated by Wasserstein GAN with added transport cost; cost function used in experiments: squared L1; compares with counterfactual fairness (causal model) and FairTest; provides transparency report as feature-difference rankings for flipsets.
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attribute defining groups S and S′ (examples: race (black/white), gender (women/men), age; privileged/unprivileged framing depends on application).
QA notes: Black-box fairness testing using optimal transport (GAN approximation) to generate in-distribution counterfactual-like pairs; introduces flipsets + transparency reports; shows can reveal subgroup discrimination even when demographic parity / equalized odds hold; stresses that flipsets are evidence for investigation, not proof of unlawful discrimination (e.g., business necessity).
Spec snippet: Definition 1 (Flipset): “F(h,G) = {x ∈ S | h(x) ≠ h(G(x))}” (with F+ and F− partitions).
Validation snippet: “Evaluating the approach on three case studies… identify subgroups that may be harmed… including in cases where the model satisfies group fairness criteria.”

DES snippet: We co-designed an AI fairness checklist and identified desiderata and concerns for AI fairness checklists in general.
ENV snippet: We found that AI fairness efforts are often the result of ad-hoc processes, driven by passionate individual advocates. Organizational culture can inhibit the efficacy of these efforts by prioritizing a fast-paced development and deployment lifecycle.
Elicitation artifact: Fairness notions list, Other, Requirements list, Scenarios
Elicitation snippet: We conducted an iterative co-design process with 48 practitioners... Through a series of semi-structured interviews and co-design workshops, we co-designed an AI fairness checklist...
Failure modes: Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Co-designs a lifecycle-structured AI fairness checklist with practitioners and surfaces organizational and sociocultural barriers, but does not operationalize fairness into measurable metrics/thresholds or specify monitoring triggers and revision governance for maintaining fairness requirements over time.
Monitoring signals: Monitoring fairness criteria after deployment (discussed as checklist items / practitioner challenge).
Monitoring snippet: Participants... shared that monitoring fairness criteria after deployment would be challenging for their teams... prompting teams to monitor fairness criteria after deployment...
Protected attrs / groups: Demographic groups such as race, gender, and age are discussed as examples of stakeholder groups; the study itself analyzes practitioner roles rather than a single protected attribute dataset.
QA notes: Qualitative, practitioner-centered paper: iterative co-design (interviews + workshops) producing a fairness checklist and themes about organizational infrastructure, workflow alignment, customization, and risks of checklists as compliance. No quantitative fairness metrics, thresholds, or decision rules are specified; monitoring/revision are discussed as challenging and as checklist prompts rather than as implemented governance mechanisms.
SOC snippet: Practitioners believe that checklists could provide organizational infrastructure for formalizing ad-hoc processes and empowering individual advocates, but only if they are aligned with teams’ existing workflows and supported by organizational culture.
Spec snippet: This initial checklist included items to consider at six different stages of the AI development and deployment lifecycle... such as “Define fairness criteria,” and “Assess fairness criteria.”
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Users
Validation snippet: We conducted an iterative co-design process with 48 practitioners... semi-structured interviews and co-design workshops...

DES snippet: We propose a scalable gradient-based algorithm called Adversarial Discrimination Finder (ADF) for generating individual discriminatory instances... ADF has two parts: global generation (clustering + gradient-guided perturbation) and local generation (gradient saliency-based neighbor search).
ENV snippet: One desirable property of DNN for applications with societal impact is fairness (i.e., non-discrimination)... discrimination is often defined over a set of protected attributes... application-dependent and given in advance.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Proposes a scalable white-box fairness testing method (ADF) to generate many individual discriminatory instances for DNNs and show retraining reduces measured individual discrimination, but provides no acceptance thresholds, stakeholder elicitation, or deployment-time monitoring and revision governance to maintain fairness requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Generate discriminatory instance pairs via ADF (global: K-means clustering + gradient-guided perturbations; local: gradient-absolute-value saliency for low-impact feature perturbation). Evaluate effectiveness by #GDiff and #ID under fixed budgets/time; evaluate efficiency by time to generate 1000 discriminatory instances; evaluate fairness improvement by retraining with generated instances and re-estimating discrimination rate via random sampling.
Metrics used: Individual discrimination rate / frequency: proportion of sampled inputs that are individual discriminatory instances (as in AEQUITAS evaluation); also counts of individual discriminatory instances (#ID) and explored non-duplicate inputs (#GDiff).
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attributes explicitly tested: age, race, gender (application-dependent; provided in advance).
QA notes: Technical SE paper: defines individual discrimination formally; introduces gradient-based ADF (global+local generation) for DNN; extensive empirical evaluation vs AEQUITAS/SG; demonstrates fairness improvement via data augmentation + retraining. No stakeholder/context elicitation; fairness target is nondiscrimination via protected-attribute counterfactual pairs; no thresholds or monitoring/revision lifecycle mechanisms.
Spec snippet: Definition 2.1 (individual discrimination): x is discriminatory if ∃x′ differing only in protected attributes P, equal on non-protected NP, and D(x) ≠ D(x′).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other
Validation snippet: Experiments on 6 benchmarks using 3 datasets (Census Income, German Credit, Bank Marketing) and protected attributes (age/race/gender) comparing ADF to AEQUITAS and Symbolic Generation on effectiveness (#ID/#GDiff), efficiency (time), and fairness improvement via retraining.

DES snippet: Defines parity-based metrics including the 80% rule / p%-rule and statistical (demographic) parity; contrasts with equalized odds / equality of opportunity, individual fairness, causal fairness, and threshold tests.
ENV snippet: Parity-based fairness advances the intrinsically valuable ideal of equality, can rectify societal bias, can prevent bias from unfair class labels, and has economic and legal benefits in some contexts.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, Other
Fairness notions: Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a structured, cross-disciplinary argument and guideline checklist for when parity-based fairness metrics are appropriate, but does not operationalize end-to-end lifecycle governance (monitoring signals, triggers, ownership) or empirical acceptance criteria beyond disparate-impact style thresholds.
Measurement procedure: Defines outcome parity via ratios (p%-rule) and distributional distance (statistical parity); discusses use as penalties/constraints in training and selecting hyperparameters to bound accuracy loss (e.g., keep validation accuracy within ~95% of baseline).
Metrics used: p%-rule / disparate impact ratio (80% / four-fifths rule); statistical/demographic parity (TV distance); equalized odds; equality of opportunity; individual fairness; counterfactual fairness; threshold tests; subgroup/intersectional parity (subgroup fairness, differential fairness)
Notes: Trade-offs managed set to Unclear per coding rule (count trade-offs only when empirically operationalized/measured).
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses protected attributes such as gender, race, nationality, sexual orientation, age, social class, political affiliation; also intersectional subgroups (e.g., Black women).
QA notes: Conceptual/position paper on whether parity-based fairness is desirable. Strong for defining parity metrics (p%-rule, statistical parity) and articulating arguments pro/contra, including infra-marginality and confounding critiques, subgroup gerrymandering, tokenism/self-fulfilling prophecy, and legal disparate impact framing. Provides a 12-item guideline checklist (RE-relevant decision artifact) but no empirical evaluation or concrete monitoring/revision mechanism.

DES snippet: Proposes simulation studies (MDP agent–environment loop) as a design/evaluation framework for long-term fairness; implements three simulated domains (lending, attention allocation, college admissions) and compares policies (e.g., max-utility vs equality of opportunity; greedy vs fairness-constrained allocation).
ENV snippet: Motivates fairness in high-impact decision ecosystems where long-term dynamics, feedback loops, and counterfactual outcomes matter; argues static fairness evaluations on fixed datasets miss key consequences of deployment over time.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, Other
Fairness notions: Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Other, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Demonstrates via simulation that fairness properties and audits can break under feedback dynamics (e.g., per-step EO not matching aggregate TPR, discovered vs missed incident non-equivalence) and provides an extensible simulation framework, but does not specify deployment-time monitoring triggers/ownership or acceptance thresholds for operational fairness requirements.
Measurement procedure: Runs repeated multi-step simulations (toy environments) for three scenarios: (1) dynamic lending with group credit score evolution; compare max-utility fixed threshold vs EO agent with group-conditional thresholds; evaluate long-run credit distributions, loans granted, and aggregate TPR gap. (2) attention allocation with incident rates responding to allocations; compare uniform, proportional, and greedy fairness-constrained agents; measure discovered vs missed incidents and discovery-probability disparity over history. (3) strategic manipulation college admissions game; compare static, robust, and continuously retrained agents; measure accuracy and group social burden at equilibrium across noise regimes. Provides ml-fairness-gym framework to reproduce/extend simulations.
Metrics used: Equality of opportunity (equal TPR per step) in lending; aggregate TPR gap (shows mismatch under dynamics); equality of discovery probability in attention allocation measured as max gap in discovered/occurred incidents across sites; welfare metrics include discovered and missed incidents; strategic manipulation setting uses accuracy and social burden (cost borne by eligible candidates), with group-specific cost functions.
Notes: Counts trade-offs as Yes (explicitly analyzes long-run welfare/fairness and objective misalignment; e.g., maximize discovered vs minimize missed incidents, access-to-loans vs credit-gap, accuracy vs social burden).
Protected attrs / groups: Uses group membership A in lending (two groups with shifted initial credit distributions); attention allocation uses sites/locations as subpopulations; college admissions uses two applicant groups with different manipulation costs (disadvantaged group pays higher cost). References vulnerable populations generally.
QA notes: FAccT’20 conceptual + simulation paper arguing fairness is dynamic and static dataset evaluations are insufficient. Introduces simulation/MDP framing and ml-fairness-gym. Three scenarios: dynamic lending (max-util vs EO per-step constraint; aggregate metric mismatch), attention allocation with dynamic incident rates (caught vs missed incidents; equality of discovery probability), and strategic manipulation admissions (accuracy vs social burden; equilibria under retraining and noise). Strong on operationalization of dynamics and evaluation via simulation; lacks concrete RE artifacts for deployment governance (monitoring triggers, revision ownership) and does not provide acceptance criteria for real systems.
SOC snippet: Highlights sociotechnical feedback effects: decisions shape the environment and power structures; warns auditing can fail when only discovered outcomes are observable (missed incidents unmeasured) and when aggregate metrics misrepresent per-step constraints (Simpson’s paradox).
Spec snippet: Defines long-term fairness evaluation as measuring realized consequences over time in simulations (agent–environment MDP); uses specific fairness objectives like equality of opportunity per step and equality of discovery probability, but positions them as evaluation lenses rather than requirements with deployment acceptance thresholds.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Uses fairness constraint parameters and thresholds in simulations (e.g., EO decision thresholds vary over time; attention allocation uses δ constraint on discovery-probability gap and epsilon-greedy exploration ε; dynamic factor d in incident-rate update; college admissions threshold θ). Numeric values are scenario-specific and varied in experiments.
Validation snippet: Simulation studies across three canonical scenarios; compares agents/policies and shows static fairness conclusions can differ from long-run dynamics; provides open-source ml-fairness-gym code for reproducibility.

DES snippet: Presents LiFT (LinkedIn Fairness Toolkit) architecture and system design for scalable fairness measurement integrated across the ML lifecycle (before/during/after training and online serving), including distributed computation (Spark), caching, and configurable APIs/UDF extensibility.
ENV snippet: Frames fairness risks in web-scale ML applications trained on biased feedback/judgments and deployed in search/recommendation and safety products; emphasizes real-world constraints: large distributed datasets, production pipelines, and fairness drift over time.
Elicitation snippet: Explicitly calls for fairness decisions to be guided by social, ethical, and legal dimensions and inputs from a diverse set of key stakeholders; discusses stakeholder alignment when metrics conflict (e.g., precision vs recall trade-off).
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, Monitoring without triggers, Other, Unstable subgroup definitions
Fairness notions: Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides an end-to-end, production-oriented framework (LiFT) for scalable fairness measurement integrated across the ML lifecycle with statistical significance testing and monitoring signals, but leaves key governance questions (who sets fairness thresholds, how to resolve conflicting metrics, and when/how to trigger mitigation or rollback) largely to organizational stakeholder processes rather than specifying concrete operational requirements.
Measurement procedure: Implements Spark-based pipeline: join labels/scores with protected-attribute dataset, build cached virtual dataset, compute distributions/benefit vectors, then compute fairness metrics either single-node (on aggregated Distribution/BenefitMap) or distributed (on cached dataset). Supports pre- and post-training measurement and (limited) mitigation via preprocessing reweighting; includes permutation testing using sampling (≈100k points) for power. Evaluated on 3 LinkedIn production pipelines (D1/D2 classifiers; D3 ranking model) plus UCI Adult dataset (oversampled to 10M) to show scalability and interpretability trade-offs among metrics.
Metrics used: Training data: representativeness and label-distribution metrics including KL divergence, Jensen–Shannon divergence, Lp norms, total variation distance, and label-distribution demographic parity δ_DP. Post-training/model: predicted-score distribution distances; equalized odds δ_EO; performance metrics across groups (AUC/AUROC, accuracy, PPV/precision, TPR/recall, FPR/FNR); aggregate inequality metrics (Generalized Entropy Index, Theil L/T) over benefit vectors; permutation tests for statistically significant subgroup differences (p-values).
Monitoring signals: Ongoing fairness metric measurement in online serving; drift detection with respect to fairness; track trends of fairness metrics over time (e.g., GEI/EO) plus statistical tests.
Monitoring snippet: Online serving stage measures and monitors fairness metrics continuously, supports A/B tests, and can detect model drift with respect to fairness.
Protected attrs / groups: Uses protected attributes such as gender (male/female/unknown) and country in LinkedIn deployments; discusses missing/uncertain protected attributes (volunteered vs inferred) and the need to account for uncertainty; notes additional segments (education qualifications) may be relevant.
QA notes: CIKM’20 applied research paper introducing LinkedIn Fairness Toolkit (LiFT) for scalable fairness measurement in production ML systems. Strong RE-relevant contribution: explicit lifecycle integration (pre/during/post training + online), distributed computation design (Spark, caching, hybrid single-node/distributed), configurable metrics/UDF extensibility, and statistical testing for subgroup performance gaps. Discusses practical challenges: data quality and small samples, protected attribute availability/uncertainty, impossibility trade-offs (precision/recall/FPR under prevalence differences), and need for multi-stakeholder governance. Mitigation coverage is limited (preprocessing variant), with open problems for end-to-end product fairness and streaming/nearline mitigation.
Revision snippet: Suggests iterative metric selection and model/data updates when unfairness is detected; highlights need for stakeholder consensus and notes hooks across lifecycle to enable repeated measurement and mitigation, but does not specify concrete operational owners/triggers beyond monitoring and stakeholder processes.
SOC snippet: Notes potential discrimination/harms for disadvantaged groups; stresses socio-technical governance: selecting fairness notions requires consensus across stakeholders (product, legal, policy, PR, engineering, ML) and careful handling of protected-attribute data and uncertainty.
Spec snippet: Defines deployment requirements for fairness tooling: flexibility (library + production workflows), scalability (distributed computation), integration hooks across ML lifecycle; formalizes fairness measurements such as δ_DP(g1,g2)=|P(Y=1|G=g1)−P(Y=1|G=g2)| for label distribution and δ_EO(y,g1,g2)=|P(Ŷ=1|Y=y,G=g1)−P(Ŷ=1|Y=y,G=g2)| for equalized odds.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Uses statistical significance testing (permutation tests with p-values) and notes need for decision thresholds/CI/SE to make bias thresholds clearer; does not prescribe universal numeric thresholds (application-specific).
Validation snippet: Deployed in three LinkedIn ML pipelines (datasets up to ~28M records) and evaluated on UCI Adult; reports resource utilization (GB-hours) for fairness metric computation vs training/scoring and shows ability to compute multiple metrics at scale; provides deployment case insights and lessons learned.

DES snippet: Uses causal DAGs/structural causal models with policy interventions do(f→π) to unify dynamical fairness literature; enables simulation when dynamics known and off-policy estimation when dynamics unknown; provides empirical off-policy estimation improvements in lending.
ENV snippet: Motivates fairness in domains with long-term effects (lending, education, recommenders) and emphasizes that repeated deployment in changing environments yields long-term fairness implications distinct from short-term ones.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, Monitoring without triggers, No revision process/ownership, Other
Fairness notions: Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Individual consistency, Other, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a causal-SCM framework to specify and evaluate fairness interventions in dynamical systems (including off-policy estimation from observational data and simulation/sensitivity when dynamics are known), but does not define deployment-time monitoring/revision governance for keeping fairness requirements valid in production systems.
Measurement procedure: Defines SCMs for sequential settings (Fair-MDP, lending, repeated classification) and uses do-operator for atomic/policy interventions. Shows off-policy evaluation from observational trajectories by identifying backdoor sets and applying doubly robust estimators; validates via lending experiment (historical MAXPROF data → evaluate/learn EQOPP-type policy). Also demonstrates on-policy simulation when dynamics known, including multi-actor interventions (credit bureau) and long-term sensitivity analysis of policies under model mismatch.
Metrics used: Interventional/policy-evaluation quantities (e.g., E_{do(f_T→π)}[U] utility, E_{do(f_T→π)}[Δ_j] group score change); equal opportunity gap δEqOpp = |P(T=1|Y=1,A=0) − P(T=1|Y=1,A=1)|; demographic parity and max-profit/EO threshold policies in lending example; repeated classification/disparity amplification model; long-term outcomes XK in Fair-MDP; off-policy estimator error comparisons (regression vs doubly robust).
Notes: Trade-offs managed set to Yes (explicit fairness–utility trade-off via λ; discusses estimation bias/variance trade-offs; evaluates welfare/fairness under different interventions).
Protected attrs / groups: Uses sensitive attribute A explicitly (e.g., two groups in lending with group-specific thresholds); repeated classification uses latent groups Z with changing proportions α_k; discusses group and individual outcomes under interventions.
QA notes: ICML’20 paper proposing causal DAGs/SCMs as a unifying framework for fairness in dynamical systems. Key contribution is making policy interventions explicit and leveraging causal identification (backdoor) to improve off-policy evaluation (doubly robust estimators) from historical data, plus using SCM modularity for simulation, multi-actor interventions (credit bureau), multi-step extensions, and sensitivity analysis. Strong operationalization of causal estimands; lacks concrete post-deployment governance/monitoring process for real systems.
SOC snippet: Highlights usefulness of causal graphs for communicating assumptions to non-technical stakeholders/policy makers and for scrutinizing assumptions in fairness-sensitive settings; discusses regulators and ethical constraints that make online testing of policies difficult.
Spec snippet: Frames fairness evaluation/learning in dynamical systems as estimating interventional quantities under policy interventions (e.g., E_{p^{do(f_T→π)}}[U], E_{p^{do(f_T→π)}}[Δ_j], δEqOpp) using SCMs; focuses on causal estimands rather than requirements statements for deployment.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Group-specific thresholds τ_j for lending policies; fairness–utility trade-off hyperparameter λ in V_π = U − λ·δEqOpp; credit-bureau intervention example uses τ_CB = 600; multi-step horizon K/steps varied in simulations.
Validation snippet: Empirically compares off-policy estimators (regression vs doubly robust) in a lending SCM, showing improved estimation error and improved learned policy objective V_π; also runs on-policy simulations for extensions (credit bureau intervention, multi-step robustness/sensitivity).

DES snippet: Defines group and individual unfairness metrics (GRU/GEDU, IRU/IEDU), fairness-aware optimization with constraints GRU≤ε1 and GEDU≤ε2 (and individual variants), and a reranking algorithm combining path score and diversity score.
ENV snippet: Frames unfairness from data imbalance and user invisibility (inactive users) and notes protected attributes are hard to obtain; motivates fairness as mitigating disparity in recommendation performance and explanation diversity for disadvantaged users.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Fairness notions: Individual consistency, Other, Outcome parity, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides explicit, testable group/individual fairness metrics and a constrained reranking/optimization method for KG-based explainable recommendation (active vs inactive users), validated on multiple datasets, but does not address post-deployment monitoring, drift handling, or revision governance to keep fairness requirements valid over time.
Measurement procedure: Split users into active (top 5% by purchases) vs inactive (remaining 95%). Compute recommendation metrics (NDCG, F1) per group and disparity GRU. Compute explanation diversity via Simpson’s Index of Diversity over user–item path patterns; compute GEDU as group mean difference. Compute individual unfairness via Gini coefficient over per-user scores. Optimize 0–1 selection Q to maximize recommendation score subject to fairness constraints; then rank with combined fairness+rec score.
Metrics used: Group Recommendation Unfairness (GRU) using NDCG@K/F1 averages difference between active vs inactive; Group Explanation Diversity Unfairness (GEDU) using Simpson’s Index of Diversity; Individual unfairness via Gini coefficient (IRU for recommendation metrics, IEDU for explanation diversity); also uses ranking/recommendation scores combined with fairness score.
Notes: Uses empirical measurements (NDCG/F1 and fairness metrics) and ablations, so Trade-offs managed is set to Yes.
Protected attrs / groups: Primary grouping is user activity/visibility: active users (top 5% by purchases) vs inactive users (remaining). Discusses traditional protected attributes (gender, age, race, religion) but treats them as often unobserved and not used for grouping here.
QA notes: SIGIR’20 paper on fairness-aware explainable recommendation over knowledge graphs. Defines fairness as disparity in recommendation performance and explanation diversity between active vs inactive users, introduces group (GRU, GEDU) and individual (Gini-based IRU/IEDU) unfairness metrics, and proposes a 0–1 integer programming reranking framework with fairness constraints (ε set to baseline disparities) plus hyperparameter studies (α, β). Strong operationalization and offline evaluation on four Amazon datasets; lacks lifecycle monitoring/revision governance for fairness over time.
SOC snippet: Discusses disadvantaged/inactive users and economic motivation to improve their experience; fairness goal is to narrow disparity between user groups in a platform context, but no operational governance roles or post-deployment process is specified.
Spec snippet: Formalizes fairness-aware recommendation as 0–1 integer programming maximizing recommendation quality subject to fairness constraints (e.g., GRU(G1,G2,Q̂) ≤ ε1, GEDU(G1,G2,Q̂) ≤ ε2).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Users
Threshold value: ε1, ε2 set to baseline disparities; uses α∈[0,1] and β∈[0,1] hyperparameters (e.g., α=0.75, β=0.5 in main results).
Validation snippet: Extensive experiments on four Amazon e-commerce datasets (CDs & Vinyl, Clothing, Cell Phones, Beauty) comparing fairness-aware reranking on top of KG-based explainable recommenders (HeteroEmbed, PGPR, KGAT), reporting NDCG/F1 and fairness metrics plus ablations over α and β.

Citation: Borkan, Daniel, Lucas Dixon, Jeffrey Sorensen, Nithum Thain, and Lucy Vasserman. “Nuanced Metrics for Measuring Unintended Bias with Real Data for Text Classification.” In Companion Proceedings of the 2019 World Wide Web Conference (WWW ’19 Companion), May 13–17, 2019, San Francisco, CA, USA. ACM, 10 pages. https://doi.org/10.1145/3308560.3317593
Failure modes: No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Introduces a robust, threshold-agnostic metric suite and a large identity-labeled real-comment test set for diagnosing unintended identity bias in toxicity classifiers via score-distribution analysis, but leaves acceptance thresholds and deployment-time monitoring/revision processes unspecified.
Measurement procedure: Define unintended bias as performance variation across designated identity groups under an equality-of-odds style framing (with assumption of reliable labels). Propose 5 threshold-agnostic metrics capturing different score-distribution shifts and mis-orderings across subgroup/background. Evaluate metrics on (1) a synthetic template-based test set and (2) a new large human-labeled dataset of 1.8M online comments (450k with identity-reference labels). Demonstrate metrics by comparing two public toxicity models (Perspective API TOXICITY@1 vs TOXICITY@6) and analyzing where mitigation improves or fails (especially short vs long comments).
Metrics used: Threshold-agnostic unintended-bias evaluation suite: Subgroup AUC; BPSN AUC (Background Positive, Subgroup Negative); BNSP AUC (Background Negative, Subgroup Positive); Positive AEG and Negative AEG (Average Equality Gaps) based on TPR/TNR across all thresholds and equivalent Mann–Whitney U formulations.
Protected attrs / groups: Identity groups referenced in text (crowd-labeled): gender (male, female, transgender, other), sexual orientation, religion, race/ethnicity, disability (plus ‘other’ categories).
QA notes: Metric + evaluation dataset paper (for unintended bias in text classifiers). Strong: clear motivation (identity-linked score skew), formal metric definitions (AUC variants + AEGs) and properties, robustness to class imbalance, and validation on synthetic and large human-labeled datasets with analysis of mitigation differences across comment length. Partial: relies on assumption of reliable labels and a curated identity list; no explicit decision threshold/acceptance criterion for ‘acceptable’ bias; no post-deployment monitoring or revision governance beyond future-work suggestions.
Spec snippet: “We introduce a suite of threshold-agnostic metrics that provide a nuanced view of this unintended bias … considering the various ways that a classifier’s score distribution can vary across designated groups.”
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Users
Validation snippet: Applies the metric suite to synthetic and large human-labeled real-comment datasets and compares two deployed toxicity models (TOXICITY@1 vs TOXICITY@6), showing bias skewed toward toxicity for identity-related content and partial mitigation improvements.

Elicitation snippet: Interviews with 17 people who either “own ethics” in their formal role or have made addressing ethics within their companies/industry a personal mission, supplemented with ethnographic and textual qualitative data.
Failure modes: Governance absent, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a qualitative account of how “ethics owners” institutionalize ethics inside tech firms and shows how corporate logics (meritocracy, technological solutionism, market fundamentalism) can turn ethics into performative checklists and risk-management, but it does not specify testable fairness requirements, metrics, thresholds, monitoring signals, or revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Not described as quantitative measurement; analysis is conceptual/qualitative: ethnographic + interviews + textual sources to identify how “ethics” is institutionalized and how corporate logics shape practices and pitfalls.
Metrics used: None (no fairness metrics; discusses ethics practices such as checklists, “red teaming,” risk accounting, codes/principles).
Notes: Key findings: three dominant tech logics (meritocracy, technological solutionism, market fundamentalism) shape ethics work; highlights pitfalls: normalizing ethical mishaps (normalization of deviance; “fail fast”) and blinkered isomorphism (industry imitation + ethics-washing/cargo-cult performances).
Protected attrs / groups: Not scoped to a specific protected attribute; references societal harms including racial bias in risk assessments and facial recognition; also #metoo/#timesup (sexism/racism).
QA notes: Strong qualitative grounding (ethnographic + 17 interviews) and clear conceptual framing of “ethics owners” + corporate logics. Operationalization is discussed as organizational practices (checklists, red teaming, risk accounting, codes/principles) rather than measurable fairness requirements. No evaluation/validation of interventions and no lifecycle monitoring/revision specifics.
Spec snippet: Ethics owners develop strategies to “align everyday practices with corporate logics” and translate external norms (e.g., UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights) into internally tractable practices; ethics risks being operationalized as checklists/toolkits that imply ethics “has been done.”
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators, Users

DES snippet: We develop a (metamorphic) testing approach called TILE for checking balanced data usage ... with mm-transformations (row/column permutations, feature name shuffling, renaming values) and equivalence checking of predictors.
ENV snippet: Fairness testing aims at checking whether a classifier ... is biased ... (e.g. gender or age)... Most often, the reasons for unfairness lie in the data set used for training.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Introduces a formal, testable fairness-like requirement for the learning phase—balanced data usage—operationalized via metamorphic relations and model-equivalence checking across training-data transformations, but does not connect to protected-group fairness metrics, acceptance thresholds, or lifecycle monitoring and revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Apply metamorphic transformations to (F,T); train models on original and transformed data; check equivalence via (1) representation equality when computable and (2) equivalence testing on sampled/random/training/min-max-median-mean test inputs (INPUT_RATIO/TRAIN_RATIO); compute approximate balancedness indicators; evaluate across 14 scikit-learn classifiers plus intentionally unbalanced baselines on 4 artificial + 9 UCI real datasets.
Metrics used: Balancedness indicators bi_t and bi (transformation-specific and overall), defined as proportions of tested transformations yielding equivalent models; sensitivity rates per transformation type; percentage of test cases classified differently after transformations.
Protected attrs / groups: Not the focus: balanced data usage aims to treat all training data/features equally (invariance to ordering/naming). Uses gender/age as examples of protected attributes in conventional fairness testing discussion.
QA notes: SE testing paper reframes fairness for the learning phase as invariance to training-data/feature permutations and renamings (balanced data usage). Provides formal metamorphic relations + TILE framework, with approximate equivalence checking and empirical evaluation on scikit-learn classifiers (12/14 unbalanced). Does not operationalize fairness as protected-group outcome parity or specify thresholds/monitoring/revision processes.
Spec snippet: Definition 1: learn is balanced iff for all mm-transformations g, learn(F,T) ≡ learn(g(F,T)) (model equivalence under row/column permutations, feature-name shuffling, value renaming).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Developers, Other
Validation snippet: Applied TILE to 14 scikit-learn classifiers with 4 artificial and 9 real-world datasets; found 12/14 classifiers unbalanced under row/column permutations; additional experiments on intentionally unbalanced classifiers and on equivalence-testing sensitivity (TRAIN_RATIO/INPUT_RATIO).

DES snippet: “Our approach combines two well-established techniques - symbolic execution and local explainability for effective test case generation.”
ENV snippet: “Individual discrimination” exists when a given individual different from another only in “protected attributes” (e.g., age, gender, race, etc.) receives a different decision outcome…
Failure modes: Governance absent, No thresholds / acceptance criteria
Gap summary (1 sentence): Strong test-generation/validation for individual discrimination in black-box models, but no stakeholder elicitation, no acceptance thresholds, and no monitoring/revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Generate test cases (non-protected attribute combinations) using Symbolic Generation (SG): use seed data + KMeans clustering; derive local decision-tree path using LIME; toggle high-confidence predicates (threshold T1=0.3) for global search; when discriminatory input found, do local symbolic search; check discrimination by enumerating combinations of protected attribute values and comparing outputs.
Metrics used: Individual discrimination (pairwise counterfactual consistency: differing only in protected attribute but different decision). Success score = |Succ|/|Gen| for test generation. Path coverage (decision regions covered via decision tree approximation).
Notes: Uses LIME decision tree path as symbolic-execution ‘path’ for black-box models; uses seed data clustering (KMeans, k=4) and confidence threshold T1=0.3; local search uses ‘sticky solutions’ near discriminatory path; experiments largely on logistic regression models trained on fairness benchmarks (German credit, Adult, Bank marketing, US executions, etc.).
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attributes P (examples used in experiments: gender, age, race, sex).
QA notes: Fairness notion is individual discrimination (non-probabilistic, strict equality on non-protected attrs; notes future work for continuous attrs/correlation handling). Black-box test generation via LIME decision-tree path + symbolic toggling; compares vs THEMIS and AEQUITAS across multiple benchmarks; provides threats to validity incl. limits for high-dimensional data and dependence on LIME approximation.
Spec snippet: “a system is said to be fair if for any two valid inputs which differ only in the protected attribute are always assigned the same class”
Validation snippet: “We empirically compare our technique with the existing algorithms i.e. THEMIS, AEQUITAS and demonstrate the performance improvement…”

DES snippet: “AIF360 is designed as an end-to-end workflow with two goals: (a) ease of use and (b) extensibility.”
ENV snippet: “Fairness is an increasingly important concern as machine learning models are used to support decision making in high-stakes applications such as mortgage lending, hiring, and prison sentencing.”

Gap summary (1 sentence): Comprehensive fairness metrics+mitigation toolkit and pipeline abstractions, but it does not define domain-specific acceptance thresholds/decision rules and provides limited guidance on governance, monitoring, or revision in deployment.
Measurement procedure: Compute dataset and classification fairness metrics via Metric classes over one or two Dataset objects; evaluate mitigation algorithms in pre-/in-/post-processing pipeline (fit/transform/predict). Example evaluation uses 25 random train/val/test splits; fairness metrics computed before/after mitigation and on predicted labels; classifiers include LR/RF/NN.

Provides 70+ metrics and 9 mitigation algorithms across pre-/in-/post-processing; scikit-learn-like API; dataset abstraction with protected attributes and provenance pointer to prior dataset state.

DES snippet: Defines pairwise fairness metrics for recommendation rankings using randomized pairwise experiments (positions 2 and 3) to estimate unbiased user preferences, decomposing fairness into overall, intra-group, and inter-group pairwise fairness. Proposes a pairwise regularization term added to pointwise model training to reduce group gaps in pairwise fairness and demonstrates improvements in a large-scale production recommender.
ENV snippet: Assumes recommender systems in production can create fairness risks by systematically under-ranking groups of items, affecting visibility and engagement for content producers/providers; notes recommender evaluation is biased due to dynamics and exposure, motivating randomized experiments for unbiased preference estimates.
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Operationalizes recommender ranking fairness with experimentally measurable pairwise metrics and a training-time regularizer that closes inter-group under-ranking gaps in production, but does not state explicit acceptance thresholds or provide lifecycle monitoring/revision governance beyond periodic experimental evaluation.
Measurement procedure: Run randomized experiments on a slice of queries by showing a random pair of relevant items in positions 2 and 3 with randomized order; record which item is clicked and subsequent engagement $z$; discretize $z$ into buckets; compute pairwise fairness probabilities and decompose into intra/inter-group. Train pointwise neural ranker with additional pairwise regularizer computed over experimental pair dataset $P$ (balanced by clicked-item group) to improve inter-group pairwise fairness; evaluate in production via relative subgroup vs non-subgroup gaps.
Metrics used: Pairwise fairness / pairwise accuracy metrics (overall, intra-group, inter-group), conditioned on engagement; exposure analysis; compares subgroup vs non-subgroup (relative ratios) and analyzes relation to pointwise metrics (calibration, MSE) showing insufficiency for ranking fairness.
Protected attrs / groups: Binary sensitive attribute on items (item-group membership); study focuses on a sensitive subgroup of items (~0.2% of items) vs non-subgroup; notes user-group extension possible but not core.
QA notes: KDD’19 paper introduces pairwise fairness metrics for recommender rankings using randomized pairwise experiments and a pairwise regularization term for pointwise rankers; demonstrates large inter-group gap reduction for a sensitive item subgroup in a production recommender. Strong operationalization and empirical evaluation; lacks stakeholder elicitation, explicit acceptance thresholds, and post-deployment monitoring/revision governance details.
SOC snippet: Frames fairness as avoiding systematic under-exposure/under-ranking of item groups (e.g., demographic group posts) in sociotechnical platforms where creators/providers rely on ranking for voice and visibility; discusses that fairness evaluation depends on experimental measurement and that retrieval-stage fairness assumptions can propagate harms.
Spec snippet: Specifies pairwise fairness as equality of conditional probabilities of ranking a clicked item above a relevant unclicked item across item groups, conditioned on engagement $z$ (Definitions 1–3), and provides measurement via randomized experiments and discretized engagement buckets.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Users
Validation snippet: Evaluated on a large-scale production recommender system using randomized experiments to estimate preferences and compare fairness gaps before/after pairwise regularization; reports substantial reduction of inter-group pairwise fairness gap (e.g., ~35.6% advantage down to ~2.6% in their production case study).

DES snippet: "... define the precision disease control problem ... optimally allocating vaccines in a social network in a step-by-step fashion ..."
ENV snippet: "... comparatively little attention has been paid to understanding the fairness implications of different treatment strategies – that is, how might such strategies distribute the expected disease burden differentially across various subgroups or communities in the population?"
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria
Gap summary (1 sentence): Defines and operationalizes outcome fairness for sequential vaccine allocation in networks (equalizing expected disease burden across individuals/communities/centrality strata) via simulation + LP-constrained policies, but provides no acceptance thresholds, monitoring signals, or revision/governance process for sustaining fairness over time.
Measurement procedure: Simulate SIR dynamics on graphs (barbell, chain, scale-free, karate club) in ML Fairness Gym; evaluate policies by expected total sick days and by burden distribution across individuals, communities, and centrality groups (degree/betweenness/eigenvector). For chain graphs, formulate fairness constraints (equalized burden) as linear programs over (preemptive or 1-step precision) treatment strategies.
Metrics used: Expected disease burden / expected sick days (including expected total sick days and distributions over individuals/communities/centrality strata); probability of infection (chain graph).
Protected attrs / groups: Graph-defined subgroups/communities (community structure in networks) and role-based groups via centrality (degree, betweenness, eigenvector); also position-based risk groups (e.g., chain middle vs ends).
QA notes: Preprint (arXiv 1911.05489v1, 2019) proposing a precision contagion control environment and analyzing efficiency–fairness trade-offs in simulated networks. Fairness operationalized as distributions of expected sick days across communities and centrality groups; equalization formulated as constrained LP (preemptive vs 1-step precision). No stakeholder elicitation or real-world deployment/monitoring/revision process described.
SOC snippet: "... pursuing a coarse metric of success ... averaged over an entire population ... can ... leave parts of the population under-served."
Spec snippet: "Finding a precision policy that equalizes disease burden can be formulated as a constrained linear program ..."
Validation snippet: "We implement the simulated dynamics of this environment using the ML Fairness Gym ..."

DES snippet: Shows that ‘problem specification and operationalization’ are negotiated and elastic: teams translate goals into target variables and proxies (e.g., dealer decisions → credit score ranges → threshold at 500), and these choices determine downstream fairness properties and error burdens even before model training.
ENV snippet: Argues fairness concerns depend on upstream ‘problem formulation’ choices (target variables, proxies) and that normative assessments often take these for granted; fairness is shaped by discretionary translations of business goals into tractable ML problems, raising distinct ethical concerns across formulations.
Elicitation snippet: Conducted six months of ethnographic fieldwork including 50+ interviews across roles (data scientists, product managers, business analysts, executives) and grounded-theory coding; the project goal (‘lead quality’) was negotiated into specific target variables and proxies.
Failure modes: Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows via ethnography that fairness is strongly determined by upstream problem formulation (targets, proxies, thresholds like credit score >500) negotiated under organizational/legal constraints, but provides no formal fairness metrics, acceptance criteria, or lifecycle monitoring/revision mechanisms for maintaining fairness requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Qualitative ethnographic analysis of problem formulation work in a corporate ‘special financing’ lending project. Traces iterative translations from strategic goals to targets/proxies and shows how data availability, legal limits (FCRA), incentives, and costs drive operational definitions (e.g., dealer decisions proxy; then credit score ranges proxy; then >500 threshold) that shape fairness implications and what gets optimized/measured.
Metrics used: No formal fairness metrics; fairness is treated as normative/ethical evaluation of target/proxy choices (e.g., disparate impact depends on which target variable is chosen; mismeasurement and label bias can hide discrimination in standard performance metrics).
Notes: Use as key evidence that fairness requirements are often implicit and negotiated during problem formulation, and that ‘requirements’ are instantiated as target variables/proxies shaped by organizational constraints; connects to RQ3 by highlighting that early formulation decisions are rarely revisited critically.
Protected attrs / groups: Does not operationalize protected groups directly; discusses protected characteristics (race/gender) as potentially correlated with target variables and as sources of measurement bias (e.g., arrests as proxy for crime with racial bias), and uses lending/admissions examples where formulations can create disparate impact.
QA notes: Ethnographic study of how fairness-relevant requirements emerge upstream through problem formulation (targets/proxies) in corporate data science. Strong for RQ1/RQ2 at ENV/SOC levels: shows discretionary, negotiated specification and operationalization, shaped by org incentives, data constraints, and legal limits. Provides an operational example (credit score >500 threshold) but does not define formal fairness metrics, acceptance criteria, monitoring, or revision mechanisms.
SOC snippet: Based on six months ethnographic fieldwork with a corporate data science team, highlighting the multi-actor negotiation (data scientists, product managers, business analysts, executives) and organizational constraints (data availability, legal constraints like FCRA, costs, incentives) that shape fairness-relevant requirements in practice.
Spec snippet: Demonstrates how high-level goals (‘improve lead quality’, ‘financeability’) were iteratively specified into tractable ML targets and proxies (e.g., predicting dealer-specific financeability; later classifying credit score above/below 500 as proxy for financeability), altering what “fairness” would mean and who is affected.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Developers, Org leadership, Other
Threshold value: Illustrative operational threshold in case study: credit score > 500 used to define ‘financeable’ leads (binary classification proxy) after collapsing heterogeneous credit-score ranges.
Validation snippet: Empirical evidence comes from ethnographic fieldwork and grounded-theory analysis (interviews, field notes) rather than quantitative model evaluation; validates claims by tracing decisions and negotiations across an observed project lifecycle.

DES snippet: Defines preference guarantees for decoupled classifiers: rationality R_z(ĥ_z) ≤ R_z(ĥ_0) (vs pooled) and envy-freeness R_z(ĥ_z) ≤ R_z(ĥ_{z′}) (vs other groups), in terms of true risk/generalization error; proposes recursive decoupling over sensitive attributes with finite-sample conditions (Theorem 2) and a grow+prune tree procedure; trains linear models via 0-1 loss minimization using integer programming.
ENV snippet: Argues that in domains like medicine, using sensitive attributes (treatment disparity) can be acceptable when aligned with ethical principles: beneficence (do the best for each group) and non-maleficence (do no harm), so any group-specific modeling should be in each group’s best interest.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides explicit, testable preference-based fairness requirements (rationality and envy-freeness) and an adaptive decoupling procedure with finite-sample guarantees and empirical validation, but does not address deployment-time monitoring, drift handling, or revision governance to keep these requirements valid over time.
Measurement procedure: Train pooled classifier ĥ_0 and candidate decoupled classifiers for groups; ensure training-time preference satisfaction via direct 0-1 loss minimization (Remark 1). Provide finite-sample sufficient condition for generalization-error preference guarantees (Theorem 2). Learn an adaptive decoupling tree by recursively splitting on feasible sensitive attributes and scoring splits by a bound on preference-violation probability. Prune candidate trees using hold-out set with McNemar tests for rationality/envy-freeness, control multiple testing with Bonferroni, and select remaining tree minimizing a cost (e.g., worst-case group risk).
Metrics used: Group-wise true risk (generalization error) R_z(h) and empirical risk Ŕ_z(h); preference gaps Δ_z(h,h′)=R_z(h)−R_z(h′) and empirical gaps; count of preference violations tested via McNemar test; worst-case group risk max_z R_z(h_a(z)) and population risk Σ_z π_z R_z(h_a(z)) as optional objectives.
Notes: Maps well to RQ2: explicit requirements as generalization-error constraints; also provides concrete acceptance testing procedure (McNemar + Bonferroni) that can be interpreted as a validation oracle. Limited RQ3 coverage: no post-deployment monitoring/revision; assumes static data distributions.
Protected attrs / groups: Sensitive attributes such as gender, ethnicity, age; experiments include multiple sensitive attributes (e.g., Adult: gender, immigrant, marital status; COMPAS recidivism; medical datasets for sleep apnea and lung cancer) with atomic intersectional groups Z.
QA notes: Strong preference-based fairness operationalization (rationality + envy-freeness) grounded in beneficence/non-maleficence; explicit, testable constraints in terms of group generalization error; adaptive decoupling procedure with statistical tests to detect preference violations and guard against intersectional gerrymandering; empirical validation on multiple datasets. Does not cover deployment monitoring or revision governance under distribution shift.
SOC snippet: Frames fairness as group preference satisfaction under potential reporting/misreporting of sensitive attributes; warns about ‘gerrymandering’ across intersectional groups and uses atomic-group preference constraints to prevent harm to small subgroups; uses hypothesis testing (McNemar + Bonferroni) on hold-out/test data to detect preference violations.
Spec snippet: Preference-based fairness requirements: (Rationality) R_z(ĥ_z) ≤ R_z(ĥ_0) for all z; (Envy-freeness) R_z(ĥ_z) ≤ R_z(ĥ_{z′}) for all z,z′, where R_z is true (generalization) error for group z. These constraints formalize ‘fairness without harm’ under beneficence/non-maleficence.
Threshold value: Preference constraints are thresholded at 0 (no worse error): Δ_z(assigned, pooled) ≤ 0 and Δ_z(assigned, other) ≤ 0. Uses significance level 10% in pruning/tests; sample complexity condition in Theorem 2 requires n_z ≥ (64 ln|H| + 4 ln(2|Z|^2/δ))/ε̂_z^2 and ε̂_z>0.
Validation snippet: Proves finite-sample conditions for preference guarantees (generalization-error bounds) and validates empirically on several real-world datasets (Adult, COMPAS, sleep apnea, lung cancer), reporting preference-violation counts on test data (McNemar, 10% level) and accuracy gains without violating preferences.

DES snippet: Formalizes many ML goals as explicit rate constraints on predictions (e.g., positive rate ≥ 0.8; statistical parity, equal opportunity/odds, minimum recall/accuracy, churn, etc.) and introduces proxy-Lagrangian constrained optimization for non-convex, non-differentiable constraints, yielding stochastic solutions with feasibility/optimality guarantees and a shrinking step to a mixture of ≤ m+1 deterministic models.
ENV snippet: Positions rate constraints as a practical interface to meet “societal, legal, and practical goals” and argues constraints have an absolute meaning that better reflects practitioner intent than weighted penalties, supporting easier verification of whether goals were achieved.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides an explicit, testable constraint-based specification of fairness and other policy goals (rate constraints) plus optimization/verification procedures with theoretical guarantees and extensive experiments, but does not address post-deployment monitoring, drift handling, or revision governance to keep such constraints valid over time.
Measurement procedure: Specify m rate constraints on datasets/slices D_k using indicator-based rates. Train models by constrained optimization: (i) Lagrangian game for non-convex constraints (mixed equilibria) with oracle-based θ-player and regret-minimizing λ-player; (ii) proxy-Lagrangian non-zero-sum game to handle non-differentiable constraints using proxy constraints for θ-player and original constraints for λ-player, converging to semi-coarse correlated equilibrium. Produce stochastic classifier (mixture over iterates) and shrink support to ≤ m+1 deterministic solutions via LP. Evaluate error and max constraint violation on train/validation/test across multiple datasets and constraints.
Metrics used: Rate constraints over prediction rates p_+(D;θ), p_-(D;θ); fairness metrics expressible as rate constraints: statistical/demographic parity, equal opportunity, equal odds; plus “no lost benefits”, “not worse off”, minimum coverage/accuracy/recall; churn and loss-only churn; win-loss ratio; constraint violation magnitudes measured on train/valid/test.
Notes: Useful for RQ2: shows how fairness requirements can be specified as constraints with absolute meaning (vs penalty weights) and verified via constraint violation; also provides group-specific policy goals like ‘not worse off’ and ‘no lost benefits’ that resemble change-control requirements for model updates, but still pre-deployment/offline.
Protected attrs / groups: Fairness constraints applied to protected groups (e.g., gender, race; Adult and COMPAS use Black/White and Male/Female; Bank Marketing uses age quantiles). Also supports group/slice constraints more generally (countries, business type, etc.).
QA notes: Core contribution is a requirements-like interface for ML via explicit, testable rate constraints (including fairness constraints) and practical optimization for non-differentiable constraints. Strong operationalization: constraints over slices/groups, concrete slack/threshold examples, and verification via constraint-violation reporting. Provides both theory (game-theoretic equilibria, feasibility/optimality, support shrinking) and extensive offline experiments. Does not provide deployment monitoring or revision governance; focus is training-time constraint satisfaction and evaluation generalization.
SOC snippet: Emphasizes practitioner-facing specification/verification of constraints across slices (groups, locales) and discusses policy-style goals like “no lost benefits” and “not worse off” for each group vs a baseline classifier, reflecting governance concerns about not harming important subsets during model updates, though without runtime monitoring governance.
Spec snippet: Defines constrained ERM: minimize empirical loss subject to m inequality constraints g_i(θ) ≤ 0, including rate constraints of the form Σ_k α_k p_+(D_k;θ) + β_k p_-(D_k;θ) ≤ κ (and examples like p_+(D[y=1];θ) ≥ 0.95 p_+(D[y=1];θ) for equal opportunity-style constraints).
Threshold value: Example thresholds include: positive rate ≥ 0.8; equal opportunity at 95% (multiplicative slack 0.95) on Adult/COMPAS; additive slacks e.g., 2% demographic parity slack (Bank Marketing), 5% additive slack in COMPAS variant; minimum accuracy on steering examples 0.67; significance/selection heuristic uses max loss-rank vs constraint-violation rank (Eq. 18).
Validation snippet: Provides theoretical guarantees for approximate feasibility/optimality via (proxy-)Lagrangian game equilibria and proves existence/shrinking to ≤ m+1 support; extensive experiments on benchmark (Adult, COMPAS, Bank Marketing) and real-world datasets showing constraint satisfaction/violations and accuracy trade-offs.

DES snippet: Defines a group fairness constraint based on statistical parity of selection rates on unlabeled data (difference in mean predicted positive probability between protected and unprotected groups) and uses it as a differentiable loss term in semi-supervised training (posterior regularization / generalized expectation) to improve both fairness and accuracy under label and selection bias.
ENV snippet: Frames fairness as an externally supplied rule (e.g., government/legal standards) imposed on learning algorithms; discusses protected attributes (race, gender, religion) and references legal grounding such as U.S. 4/5ths rule, highlighting how evaluation practices must account for label/selection bias when interpreting fairness–accuracy trade-offs.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows that apparent fairness–accuracy trade-offs can disappear when evaluation accounts for label/selection bias and proposes a semi-supervised fairness-constraint method that can improve both, but does not provide deployment-time monitoring or explicit acceptance thresholds/governance for maintaining fairness requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Simulate datasets with controlled label bias (y sampled from z with bias parameter α conditioned on protected attribute) and controlled selection bias in training set (parameter β); evaluate classifiers by sweeping fairness enforcement strength and plotting fairness vs accuracy when accuracy is computed on biased vs unbiased ground truth; propose semi-supervised objective combining supervised NLL on labeled data with fairness loss on unlabeled data, optimized via SGD in standard toolkits.
Metrics used: Fairness measured by selection rate ratio ḡ_w(D^P)/ḡ_w(D^U) (perfect fairness=1). Accuracy measured via weighted macro F1, evaluated against both biased labels y and simulated/unbiased labels z. Studies fairness–accuracy curves under varying label-bias parameter α and selection-bias parameter β; compares supervised baseline, in-processing fairness regularizer, reweighing pre-processing, and proposed semi-supervised method.
Notes: Useful synthesis point: shows why fairness requirements tied to biased labels can create misleading acceptance decisions; supports your ‘specification + validation must align’ argument. Treat as ‘evaluation validity’ rather than deployment monitoring.
