Protected attrs / groups: Binary protected attribute ρ representing protected vs unprotected groups; examples include race, gender, religion. Experiments use simulated data and COMPAS-derived data with protected attribute and biased/unbiased labels (y vs latent unbiased z).
QA notes: Key paper for your RQ2/RQ3 framing around ‘requirements validity’ and evaluation: argues fairness–accuracy trade-off can be an evaluation artifact when labels are biased; introduces controlled simulation with latent unbiased labels to evaluate accuracy properly. Provides explicit, testable operationalization of demographic parity selection-rate constraints and a semi-supervised method that uses fairness constraints on unlabeled data to improve both fairness and accuracy under label and selection bias. No monitoring/revision/governance mechanisms beyond offline evaluation; assumptions (“we’re all equal”) are critical and may not hold.
Spec snippet: Uses a demographic-parity-style constraint on selection rates: want ḡ_w(D^P_un)/ḡ_w(D^U_un) close to 1 (parity), relaxed to a differentiable loss C(D_un,w) = (p̄_w(D^P_un) − p̄_w(D^U_un))^2 with parity achieved at 0, applied over large unlabeled datasets.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Mentions U.S. 4/5ths rule as legal grounding for statistical parity selection-rate constraints (no explicit numeric threshold used as acceptance criterion in experiments; fairness target is ratio≈1 / loss=0).
Validation snippet: Empirical study on simulated data (Bayesian network generator) and COMPAS-derived data, varying label/selection bias and comparing baselines; reports mean and standard error over repeated runs/splits.

DES snippet: Extends equalized odds to cardinality-constrained classification/resource allocation (allocate k units) and provides a geometric characterization/algorithm for the optimal equalized-odds allocation under the cardinality line intersection with the maximum curve; defines efficiency as number of true positives under fixed k; proves compatibility/incompatibility results between equalized odds and fair-division axioms.
ENV snippet: Motivates fairness requirements for resource allocation under algorithmic decision-making (loans, admissions, parole) and argues that limited resources (exactly k positives) are ubiquitous, so fairness notions must be compatible with ‘paradox-avoiding’ relational axioms from fair division (resource monotonicity, consistency, population monotonicity).
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows that under explicit resource/cardinality constraints, equalized odds can coexist with resource monotonicity but is severely incompatible with consistency and population monotonicity (forcing near-uniform allocations and large efficiency loss), highlighting governance-relevant paradoxes yet offering no deployment-time monitoring or revision mechanisms.
Measurement procedure: Assume calibrated bucketed risk scores per group (g,p) with counts d^p_g. Choose allocations ℓ^p_g (divisible goods/probability of receiving item) subject to per-agent cap and total k. Compute signatures (mean allocation to positives/negatives) and enforce equalized odds by intersecting implementable signature sets S_g across groups plus the cardinality line. Prove axiomatic results: resource monotonicity compatible with optimal equalized odds; consistency + equalized odds implies uniform allocation; population monotonicity + equalized odds cannot approximate optimal EO by constant factor. Empirically evaluate efficiency loss on FICO dataset across k values.
Metrics used: Equalized odds expressed via equal group TPR/FPR (in allocation form: equal mean allocation among positives and among negatives across groups). Efficiency objective is total allocation to positives (true positives) under fixed cardinality k. Evaluates efficiency loss (fraction of unfair optimum) for equalized-odds allocations under additional axioms; compares to uniform allocation and an upper bound for population monotonicity.
Notes: Strong for RQ2 and RQ3 framing: shows that fairness constraints can conflict with ‘stability across instances’ requirements (consistency/population monotonicity), creating paradoxes that matter for governance/change scenarios. Still treated as offline allocation rule design; no runtime monitoring or revision process.
Protected attrs / groups: Groups are protected subpopulations; empirical evaluation uses race groups on FICO credit scores (white, black, Hispanic, Asian) with buckets over credit-score values and calibrated repayment probabilities.
QA notes: Bridges ML fairness (equalized odds) with fair-division relational axioms under explicit cardinality constraints, exposing paradoxes and stability requirements across allocation instances. Strong formalization and operationalization with geometric characterization and polynomial-time algorithm, plus theory results showing: resource monotonicity can be satisfied ‘for free’; consistency forces uniform allocation; population monotonicity precludes constant-factor approximation, and empirically yields near-uniform efficiency. No stakeholder elicitation or deployment-time monitoring/revision mechanisms.
SOC snippet: Interprets fairness as stability/justifiability across changing allocation instances: resource monotonicity avoids decreasing anyone’s allocation when more resources appear; consistency links allocations across subpopulations (and would preclude Simpson’s paradox-like effects); population monotonicity captures ‘no one is worse off when others leave/join’—showing that some fairness constraints can yield counter-intuitive, hard-to-justify policy outcomes.
Spec snippet: Equalized odds allocation constraints: mean allocation to positive agents is equal across groups and mean allocation to negative agents is equal across groups; i.e., allocation signatures (L^+g/D^+_g, L^-_g/D^-_g) must match across groups, subject to a global cardinality constraint Σ{g,p} ℓ^p_g = k with 0 ≤ ℓ^p_g ≤ d^p_g.
Threshold value: Cardinality constraint k (fixed number of positives/loans) is the key threshold-like parameter; allocations are threshold allocations within groups over calibrated probability buckets p, with a group threshold p* implied by the optimal equalized-odds solution for a given k.
Validation snippet: Provides theoretical proofs of compatibility/incompatibility (Theorems 2–4) and empirically evaluates efficiency loss of equalized-odds allocation rules satisfying each axiom on FICO credit score data.

DES snippet: Characterizes fair-ML as relying on abstraction/modularity (fairness metrics, constraints, portable wrappers) and identifies five ‘abstraction traps’ (Framing, Portability, Formalism, Ripple Effect, Solutionism) showing why purely technical framing/solutions can mis-specify requirements and invalidate fairness guarantees in deployment contexts.
ENV snippet: Argues that fairness, justice, and due process are properties of social and legal systems, and that abstracting away social context makes fair-ML interventions ineffective or dangerously misguided; emphasizes that social context determines what fairness means and which trade-offs are appropriate.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Fairness notions: Contestability/recourse, Contextual/legal compliance, Other, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Argues that treating fairness as a portable, formal property of a technical subsystem creates predictable ‘abstraction traps’ that can invalidate fairness guarantees in real sociotechnical contexts, motivating process- and governance-oriented requirements approaches but offering no concrete metrics, monitoring, or revision procedures.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual/analytical synthesis grounded in STS and socio-legal scholarship; explains how abstraction choices determine what is measured and which fairness guarantees hold, and why guarantees can break when sociotechnical behavior deviates from modeled assumptions (e.g., judges’ inconsistent adoption of recommendations).
Metrics used: Discusses common fairness metrics/constraints (e.g., disparate impact via EEOC 80% rule; error-rate parity; equal accuracy) and critiques treating them as complete fairness definitions. Uses illustrative ‘fairness guarantee’ examples with thresholds (e.g., disparity no more than X%) but does not propose a new metric.
Notes: Key conceptual anchor for your ENV/DES/SOC axis and for ‘validity over time’ arguments: fairness definitions are contested and shifting, and insertion of tech changes the system (ripple effects), so static requirements/metrics can lose validity.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses protected groups and demographic information in fair-ML generally; uses examples including race in criminal justice risk assessment and employment/hiring contexts; highlights that fairness criteria must be tied to the relevant human outcome (e.g., detain-or-release) rather than only predicted labels.
QA notes: Strong sociotechnical/STS critique that directly supports RQ1 and RQ3: shows why fairness requirements cannot be reduced to portable metrics/constraints and why guarantees fail when social actors and institutions are outside the abstraction boundary. Provides a process-oriented checklist and mitigation strategies, but no empirical case study or formal acceptance thresholds; no monitoring/revision mechanism beyond recommending process and documentation.
SOC snippet: Emphasizes sociotechnical framing: fairness guarantees can fail when human institutions (e.g., judges) do not follow algorithmic thresholds consistently or modify decisions in biased ways; highlights how technologies reshape incentives/power (ripple effects), and why procedural/contextual/contestable notions of fairness require processes and governance beyond metrics.
Spec snippet: Defines the core problem as an ‘abstraction error’: treating fairness/justice as properties of a technical subsystem rather than end-to-end sociotechnical outcomes; proposes five traps as failure modes and a process-oriented checklist for deciding whether and how to design fair-ML systems (considering solutionism→ripple effects→formalism→portability→framing).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Illustrative examples: EEOC ‘80% rule’ as threshold for disparate impact; hypothetical guarantee of ‘racial disparity in treatment no more than X%’ if thresholds are used. These are discussed critically rather than prescribed as acceptance criteria.
Validation snippet: Presents theory-driven analysis of abstraction failure modes (five traps) with grounding examples (risk assessment, hiring) and design-oriented mitigations; not an empirical evaluation of a specific algorithm.

DES snippet: Models direct and indirect discrimination as causal path-specific effects in a causal graph; provides criteria (SE_pd, SE_pi > t) and algorithms (PSE-DD discovery; PSE-DR removal via optimizing modified CPT of decision node under discrimination constraints) including handling unidentifiable indirect effects via upper/lower bounds.
ENV snippet: Frames anti-discrimination as a legal/ethical requirement, citing laws like the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and distinguishing direct vs indirect discrimination (e.g., redlining via ZIP code as proxy for race), motivating non-discrimination constraints on data and decisions.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a causal, testable specification of direct and indirect discrimination (path-specific effects with legal threshold t) and utility-preserving removal algorithms with bounds for unidentifiable cases, but does not address sociotechnical governance or how fairness requirements remain valid under deployment-time drift.
Measurement procedure: 1) Learn causal graph (e.g., PC algorithm via TETRAD). 2) Compute SE_pd and SE_pi using path-specific effect formulas; if recanting witness criterion holds, compute bounds ub/lb for SE_pi. 3) Discovery flags direct/indirect discrimination when SE>t. 4) Removal: solve quadratic program to modify CPT P(e|pa_E) minimizing Euclidean/χ² distance between joint distributions subject to discrimination constraints; generate modified dataset from resulting causal model; re-test discrimination and evaluate downstream classifier outcomes.
Metrics used: Causal path-specific effects (SE_pd for direct; SE_pi for indirect) computed from observational data on a learned causal graph using truncated factorization; discrimination threshold t (default 0.05; examples include 0.05 legal threshold) used as acceptance bound. Also reports utility loss as χ² distance between original and modified joint distributions; evaluates downstream classifier discrimination after preprocessing.
Notes: Maps well to your ‘requirements as testable constraints’ theme: explicit acceptance bound t and a decision rule (modify CPT) tied to measured discrimination effects. Also provides a clear example of how legal/causal notions translate into measurable requirements.
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attribute C examples include sex, race, age; redlining (unjustified) attributes R include ZIP code / marital_status / education level etc. Experiments: Adult (protected=sex, decision=income, redlining=marital_status and others in unidentifiable setting); Dutch Census (protected=sex, decision=occupation, redlining=marital_status).
QA notes: Strong RQ2 operationalization paper: formalizes discrimination as causal path-specific effects (direct vs indirect) with explicit threshold t; provides algorithms for discovery and removal with utility-preserving optimization, plus treatment of unidentifiable indirect effects via bounds (recanting witness/kite pattern). Evaluates on real datasets and checks downstream prediction discrimination. Limited on SOC/over-time: no monitoring or revision process; focuses on pre-release data remediation and offline evaluation; acknowledges future work on fair predictive models.
Spec snippet: Defines direct discrimination as pd-specific effect SE_pd(c⁺,c) along C→E and indirect discrimination as pi-specific effect SE_pi(c⁺,c) along paths through redlining attributes R; discrimination exists if SE > t for either direction, where t is a user-defined legal threshold (e.g., 0.05 in British sex-discrimination legislation).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: Discrimination threshold t: default 0.05 (varied 0.025–0.1); cites example legal threshold t=0.05. Constraints require SE_pd and SE_pi ≤ t (both directions) after removal.
Validation snippet: Experiments on Adult and Dutch Census datasets evaluate discrimination discovery/removal, utility loss, and whether classifiers trained on modified data still produce discriminatory predictions; also analyzes unidentifiable situations via bounds and compares refined vs crude removal.

DES snippet: Describes a deployed data validation system (part of TFX/TF Data Validation) with three components: Data Analyzer (summary statistics), Data Validator (schema-based anomaly detection + suggested schema updates), and Model Unit Tester (schema-guided synthetic data / fuzz testing to catch training-code assumptions). Supports single-batch validation, inter-batch validation (drift + training-serving skew), and model unit testing workflows.
ENV snippet: Assumes production ML pipelines where training and serving data evolve continuously and can contain structured, code-induced errors (schema-free data, training-serving skew, drift) that ML models may “soldier on” through; emphasizes feedback loops where serving logs become training data, amplifying small data bugs.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Monitoring without triggers, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Presents a production-deployed, schema-driven data validation and model unit-testing framework (TFDV/TFX) that operationalizes continuous monitoring for anomalies, drift, and training-serving skew with actionable alerts and schema co-evolution, but it does not explicitly connect data validation to fairness/protected-group harms or define governance beyond schema ownership/versioning.
Measurement procedure: Compute per-batch summary statistics (Data Analyzer). Validate each batch vs schema constraints (Data Validator) to flag anomalies and suggest schema updates as data evolves. For inter-batch checks, compare training vs serving and successive batches: feature-level joins for feature skew, distribution distance for distribution skew, including estimation with Dirichlet priors. Use schema to generate synthetic data for fuzz-style unit tests of training code to reveal mismatches between code assumptions and schema/data. Report production deployment evidence and case studies.
Metrics used: Operational metrics: anomaly categories and alerting; distribution distance metrics (d∞ and also L1/d1 discussed) for drift/skew; empirical validation from deployment (e.g., schema edit counts across pipelines; anomaly firing/fix rates; unit-test execution counts and failure rate; A/B win from removing skew).
Monitoring signals: Schema violations (missing/new features, type/domain violations, value-count issues), training-serving skew (feature/value mismatches), distribution drift/skew (d∞/d1 distances), and model unit-test failures indicating training-code assumptions not reflected in schema.
Monitoring snippet: System continuously validates each incoming training/serving batch against schema, monitors training-serving skew and distribution drift, and generates anomaly alerts plus suggested schema edits; emphasizes catching errors early before they propagate through feedback loops.
QA notes: SysML 2019 paper describing TensorFlow Data Validation within TFX. Strong operational RE relevance: explicit schema constraints, monitoring signals, drift/skew metrics, and an iterative human-in-the-loop workflow (schema inference + suggested edits + versioning) plus schema-guided fuzz/unit tests for training code. Empirical support comes from production deployment stats and case studies rather than controlled experiments. Fairness is not a primary focus, but the work is directly about monitoring/validation and preventing silent failures in ML pipelines.
Revision snippet: Schema is inferred initially then manually curated and evolves with data via user-reviewed suggested edits; stored in version control and treated as a production asset, with iterative updates when anomalies reflect natural data evolution (otherwise code fixes are required).
SOC snippet: Frames data validation as necessary for reliability and safety of ML systems, reducing outages and engineering burden; focuses on operational accountability via high-precision, actionable alerts to on-call engineers (spammy/non-actionable alerts are ignored), but does not directly address protected groups or discrimination.
Spec snippet: Codifies expected data properties via an ML-oriented schema (types, domains, presence, value counts, lifecycle stage, drift/skew comparators) and validates each batch against it, producing interpretable anomaly categories and suggested schema edits. For distribution drift/skew, argues standard statistical tests (e.g., chi-square) are too sensitive at scale and uses interpretable distribution-distance metrics like d∞(p,q)=max_i|p_i−q_i| with thresholds, with statistical estimation via Dirichlet priors. Adds schema-guided model unit tests by generating synthetic examples conforming to schema to expose hidden assumptions in training code (e.g., log of negative values).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Developers, Org leadership, Other
Threshold value: User-tuned thresholds for distribution distance (e.g., “allow changes up to 1% per value” for d∞). Confidence level example alpha=0.01 for chi-square is discussed as problematic at scale; drift/skew comparators are encoded in schema constraints.
Validation snippet: Validation includes (a) production deployment evidence at Google/TFX scale (petabytes/day; hundreds of teams), with aggregate statistics on schema co-evolution, anomaly frequencies, and unit-test failures, and (b) case studies including a 2% install-rate lift after fixing training-serving feature skew in a recommender pipeline and faster debugging/migration wins.

DES snippet: Proposes Flexibly Fair VAE (FFVAE) to learn representations that can be modified at test time (discard/noise sensitive latent dimensions) to achieve subgroup demographic parity across multiple sensitive attributes and their conjunctions; defines desiderata via independence constraints among latent subspaces and predictiveness MI alignment between sensitive attributes and designated latent dimensions.
ENV snippet: Motivates fairness as avoiding discriminatory behavior in high-stakes domains (law, finance, medicine) and notes legal/ethical constraints and practical/legal barriers to collecting sensitive attributes, motivating methods that can reduce discrimination even when sensitive attributes are unavailable at inference time.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a formal, testable operationalization of multi-attribute subgroup demographic parity via flexibly fair representations and offline audits, but does not address sociotechnical governance, deployment-time monitoring, or how fairness validity is maintained under distribution shift.
Measurement procedure: Train encoder FFVAE using multiple sensitive attributes at training time; at audit/test time, modify representation by dropping/noising sensitive latent dimensions to enforce subgroup demographic parity; evaluate via auditing scheme on held-out data: train downstream MLP on modified representation to predict held-out label y and report accuracy–DP trade-offs across groups and conjunctions; compare to baselines (β-VAE, FactorVAE, CVAE variants).
Metrics used: Demographic parity distance (DP) for group/subgroup fairness; accuracy vs DP Pareto fronts in audits; disentanglement and predictiveness audits via classifiers to test whether sensitive attribute can be predicted from designated latent b_i and not from the remainder; uses held-out labels y for downstream tasks (e.g., XPosition, violentCrimesPerCapita, HeavyMakeup).
Notes: Mark as multi-modal because evaluated on both tabular and image datasets. Note that fairness notion is primarily outcome parity (DP), not error parity. Highlights test-time adaptation without requiring sensitive attributes (useful in legally constrained settings).
Protected attrs / groups: Multiple sensitive attributes a (binary vector) and their intersections/subgroups. Experiments use (i) DSpritesUnfair: sensitive attributes Shape and Scale; (ii) Communities & Crime: racePctBlack, blackPerCapIncome, pctNotSpeakEnglWell; (iii) CelebA: Chubby, Eyeglasses, Male.
QA notes: Technical contribution: multi-attribute/subgroup fair representation learning with compositional test-time adaptation; clear formalization of demographic parity and auditing-based measurement. Strong RQ2 (operationalization) but limited on ENV/SOC: no stakeholder elicitation, governance, monitoring, or revision mechanisms; acknowledges distribution shift robustness as future work.
Spec snippet: Fairness criterion is demographic parity (ŷ ⟂ a). Uses demographic parity distance DP = |E[ŷ=1|a=1] − E[ŷ=1|a=0]|, and extends to multi-attribute/subgroup fairness by requiring independence w.r.t. each sensitive attribute and conjunctions; enables compositional adaptation by removing the corresponding latent dimensions {b_j}.
Validation snippet: Empirical evaluation on synthetic DSpritesUnfair plus real datasets Communities & Crime (tabular) and CelebA (images), reporting fairness–accuracy trade-offs across multiple sensitive attributes and subgroups.

DES snippet: Defines allocative fairness via discovery probability fi(vi)=E[disc(vi,ci)/ci] and α-fairness requiring |fi(vi)−fj(vj)|≤α; introduces discovery models (precision: disc=min(ci,vi); random: hypergeometric sampling) and learning algorithm under censored feedback using MLE (Poisson) + repeated computation of optimal α-fair allocations.
ENV snippet: Motivated by feedback loops in predictive policing (arrest data misestimates true crime rates) and frames fairness as equality of opportunity for candidates (e.g., conditional on committing a crime, arrest probability should be independent of district).
Failure modes: Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Defines explicit, testable fairness constraints for resource allocation (via discovery probability) and provides learning algorithms with finite-sample guarantees under censored feedback plus empirical evaluation on predictive policing data, but does not address stakeholder elicitation, production monitoring, or revision governance for maintaining fairness requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Models allocation as V units across G groups with unknown candidate-count distributions Ci and discovery model disc(vi,ci). Computes optimal α-fair allocations offline (Algorithm 1, greedy with tail probabilities) and learns online under censored feedback in precision model by repeatedly estimating Ci via MLE (Poisson family) and recomputing optimal α-fair allocation (Algorithm 2), with finite-sample bounds on parameter error and resulting (α+4D)-fairness/utility gap (D=TV distance).
Metrics used: Discovery probability per group fi(vi)=E[disc(vi,ci)/ci]; fairness violation as max_{i,j}|fi−fj|; utility χ(v)=Σ_i E_{ci∼Ci}[disc(vi,ci)] (expected discovered candidates). Price of Fairness PoF(α)=χ(w*)/χ(wα) (and inverse PoF in plots).
Notes: Key link to RQ3: makes feedback loops explicit and shows how a learning algorithm can avoid under-exploration under censored feedback. Still primarily pre-deployment/offline evaluation rather than production monitoring.
Protected attrs / groups: Groups are districts (predictive policing) and demographics (lending/hiring examples; e.g., racial groups, demographics on ad platform). Fairness goal: candidate-level equality of opportunity across groups.
QA notes: Strong operationalization of fairness constraints for allocation with explicit discovery model assumptions; includes learning under censored feedback and feedback-loop motivation. Empirical evaluation is simulation/offline using reported-crime data; does not provide stakeholder elicitation, deployment monitoring plan, or change-management governance.
SOC snippet: Discusses deployment-time learning from its own allocations (arrest data) creating feedback loops, and designs algorithms that provably overcome under-exploration; frames fairness constraints over districts/demographics in policing and hiring allocation contexts.
Spec snippet: Allocative fairness definition: for any α∈[0,1], allocation v is α-fair if |fi(vi,disc,Ci) − fj(vj,disc,Cj)| ≤ α for all groups i,j, where fi(vi)=E_{ci∼Ci}[disc(vi,ci)/ci].
Threshold value: Fairness slack/violation budget α (unfairness budget) varied, e.g., α in [0,0.15] in Pareto/frontier plots; examples discuss α=0.05 and α≈0.1 (10% discovery-probability difference) regimes.
Validation snippet: Empirical evaluation on Philadelphia Crime Incidents dataset: verifies Poisson fit per district, simulates censored observations under precision model, compares learned vs optimal Pareto frontiers (crimes discovered vs fairness violation), and analyzes Price of Fairness curves for different V.

DES snippet: Formalizes strategic classification with best-response feature manipulation under outcome-monotonic costs; shows any gain in institutional (strategic) utility necessarily increases social burden B+(f) on positive individuals, with threshold classifiers on outcome likelihood τ capturing the trade-off interval between non-strategic τ0 and Stackelberg τ* solutions.
ENV snippet: Motivates strategic behavior in high-stakes decisions via Goodhart/Campbell’s Law (“Once a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure”) and argues robustness against gaming can create negative externalities and fairness harms, especially for disadvantaged subpopulations.
Failure modes: Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Defines measurable constructs (social burden and social gap) showing that improving strategic robustness increases costs borne by positive individuals and can widen disparities for disadvantaged groups, but does not provide stakeholder elicitation artifacts or deployment-time monitoring/revision mechanisms to govern these requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Model: binary classifier published by institution; individuals best respond by choosing x′ maximizing u_x(f,x′)=f(x′)−c(x,x′). Assume outcome-monotonic costs so cost depends only on outcome likelihood ℓ(x)=P(Y=1|X=x). Show any classifier has equivalent outcome-likelihood threshold classifier f_τ. Prove U_Δ(τ) quasiconcave and B+(τ) non-decreasing; improving institutional utility implies higher social burden. Extend to subpopulations with group-specific burden and prove social gap increases with τ when disadvantaged in features or costs. Empirically illustrate assumptions and gaps with FICO credit score dataset (white vs black).
Metrics used: Institutional (strategic) utility U_Δ(f)=P(f(Δ(x))=y); non-strategic utility U(f)=P(f(x)=y). Social burden B+(f)=E[b_f(x) | y=1] with b_f(x)=min_{f(x′)=1} c(x,x′). Group burden B+,g and social gap G(f)=B+,b−B+,a. Uses thresholds on outcome likelihood τ (or score threshold in FICO example, e.g., τ=58 prime loan threshold) to trace trade-off curves.
Notes: Strong support for RQ3: shows a mechanism by which post-deployment adaptation (gaming) and robustness responses can shift burdens and exacerbate group disparities, motivating requirements-level acceptance criteria beyond accuracy. Focus is analytical + offline case study, not operational monitoring.
Protected attrs / groups: Race-based subpopulations in case study: a=white, b=black (FICO credit data). Also discusses disadvantaged subpopulations by gender, socioeconomic status, and other protected attributes in general framing.
QA notes: Introduces explicit operational constructs for the impact of strategy-robust classifiers on individuals (social burden) and proves an inherent trade-off between institutional strategic utility and burden. Extends to fairness by defining group burdens and social gap, proving robustness can worsen disparities under feature- or cost-based disadvantage, and illustrates with FICO (white vs black) case study. Does not provide stakeholder elicitation, monitoring, or revision governance.
SOC snippet: Defines and analyzes group-specific social burden B+,g and social gap G(f)=B+,b−B+,a; proves strategic robustness increases disparity when groups differ in feature distributions or manipulation costs; illustrates with FICO lending case study (white vs black) and discusses societal implications of raising decision thresholds.
Spec snippet: Defines social burden requirement construct: individual burden b_f(x)=min_{f(x′)=1} c(x,x′); social burden B+(f)=E[b_f(x) | y=1]. Under outcome-monotonic costs, any classifier is equivalent to an outcome-likelihood threshold classifier f_τ(x)=I{ℓ(x)≥τ}, enabling explicit trade-offs between institutional utility U_Δ(τ) and social burden B+(τ).
Threshold value: Non-strategic threshold τ0=0.5 (in likelihood space); strategic/Stackelberg optimum τ≥τ0; trade-off interval [τ0,τ]. In FICO case study, uses score threshold τ=58 for prime loans (score normalized 0–100).
Validation snippet: Theoretical proofs establish utility–burden trade-offs and monotonic social gap results; a case study on FICO credit data empirically illustrates assumptions (monotonic repayment probability vs score) and plots social gap vs threshold under linear cost models.

DES snippet: Formalizes a strategic classification Stackelberg game with two groups A (advantaged) and B (disadvantaged) where manipulation costs differ (cA(y)−cA(x) ≤ cB(y)−cB(x)); analyzes equilibrium learner classifiers and shows inevitable asymmetric error patterns under heterogeneous manipulation costs.
ENV snippet: Frames strategic manipulation as shaped by social inequality: groups face different costs to feature manipulation due to structural factors (resources, access), which can reinforce existing patterns of advantage/disadvantage in consequential decision systems.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides an explicit, formal analysis of how unequal costs of strategic manipulation produce inequality-reinforcing errors and can make even well-intentioned interventions (subsidies) harmful, but does not provide elicitation artifacts, operational fairness acceptance thresholds, or monitoring/revision governance for maintaining fairness requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Define Strategic Classification Game with Groups (Stackelberg): learner publishes classifier f; candidates manipulate features y ≥ x if it flips outcome and manipulation cost < 1; analyze equilibrium (undominated learner strategies) in 1D and dD with linear costs; characterize systematic error types (FP on advantaged group, FN on disadvantaged group) and analyze subsidy interventions (proportional/flat) via resulting equilibrium shifts and welfare effects.
Metrics used: Learner loss/penalty based on false positives and false negatives (CFP/CFN) under strategic best responses; candidate welfare modeled as payoff f(y) − (cm(y)−cm(x)) (and subsidy-adjusted costs). Group-level impacts analyzed via equilibrium error regions and welfare comparisons.
Notes: Useful primarily for RQ3/RQ4 framing: shows how post-publication agent adaptation and unequal capacity to respond can invalidate naive fairness expectations and create unintended harms; emphasizes agent welfare rather than only accuracy.
Protected attrs / groups: Abstracts groups as advantaged (A) vs disadvantaged (B); motivates with protected attributes like race and gender and resource-access differences affecting ability to manipulate features (e.g., test prep access).
QA notes: The paper contributes a formal, testable model showing that unequal manipulation costs yield systematic inequality-reinforcing classification errors (FP benefiting advantaged group; FN harming disadvantaged group) even when the learner knows costs, and that subsidy interventions can paradoxically harm both groups’ welfare. Strong conceptual/analytical contribution but no lifecycle monitoring/revision or acceptance thresholds for fairness requirements.
SOC snippet: Highlights sociotechnical consequences of deployment: publishing a classifier changes agent behavior; unequal capacity to respond yields inequality-reinforcing outcomes; considers an intervention (learner subsidies) and shows cases where subsidies improve learner utility but reduce welfare for both groups.
Spec snippet: Model requirement/assumption: for all x∈[0,1]^d and y ≥ x, manipulation cost differences satisfy cA(y)−cA(x) ≤ cB(y)−cB(x) (disadvantaged group faces higher costs). Learner objective penalizes false positives/negatives under equilibrium with strategic best responses.
Validation snippet: Provides theoretical equilibrium characterizations (propositions/theorems) showing inequality-reinforcing error structure under heterogeneous manipulation costs and constructing counterexamples where subsidies reduce welfare for both groups.

Citation: Buolamwini, Joy, and Timnit Gebru. “Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification.” In Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, vol. 81, Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAT*), 2018, pp. 1–15. (PPB dataset: http://gendershades.org/)
Failure modes: Other
Fairness notions: Contestability/recourse, Other, Outcome parity, Procedural fairness
Gap summary (1 sentence): Demonstrates large intersectional performance disparities in commercial gender classification and shows that benchmark composition hides these gaps, motivating fairness requirements for disaggregated evaluation and representative datasets, but it does not propose deployment monitoring/revision or quantitative acceptance thresholds for when performance is ‘fair enough’.
Measurement procedure: Create Pilot Parliaments Benchmark (PPB) with 1270 individuals balanced by binary gender label and Fitzpatrick skin type; annotate existing benchmarks (IJB-A, Adience) for skin type to quantify representation; evaluate 3 commercial gender classifiers (Microsoft, IBM, Face++) on PPB and report performance disaggregated by gender, skin type, and intersectional subgroups; analyze confidence scores (IBM) and discuss dataset/measurement implications.
Metrics used: Audit/benchmark metrics: subgroup error rate (1−TPR), TPR, FPR, PPV, overall accuracy; intersectional subgroup breakdown (darker female, darker male, lighter female, lighter male) using Fitzpatrick skin type (I–VI aggregated to lighter I–III vs darker IV–VI).
Protected attrs / groups: Gender (binary labels used by APIs: female/male) and skin type (Fitzpatrick I–VI; aggregated to lighter vs darker). Discusses race/ethnicity contextually (e.g., African-American individuals in policing use cases) but primary measured attribute is skin type.
QA notes: Seminal algorithmic audit/benchmarking paper. Strong on problem framing (intersectional harms), operationalization (Fitzpatrick skin type + subgroup metrics), and empirical evaluation of commercial systems with clear disparity magnitudes. Partial: does not define formal fairness acceptance criteria or remediation process; limited lifecycle governance (monitoring/revision) beyond calling for transparency/accountability; uses binary gender labels per available APIs.
Spec snippet: “The substantial disparities in the accuracy of classifying darker females, lighter females, darker males, and lighter males … require urgent attention if commercial companies are to build genuinely fair, transparent and accountable facial analysis algorithms.”
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Auditors/Compliance, Developers, Regulators, Users
Validation snippet: Evaluates commercial API classifiers on PPB and reports subgroup performance gaps (e.g., darker-skinned female error rates up to 34.7% vs lighter-skinned male max 0.8%).

Elicitation snippet: ...producing fair software requires (1) eliciting and specifying requirements that capture fairness properties...
Gap summary (1 sentence): Vision/call-to-action: fairness as a first-class software property; highlights RE/design/testing/verification challenges but provides no concrete operationalization or acceptance criteria.
QA notes: Vision paper (6 pages) framing fairness defects across lifecycle; proposes research agenda (catalog of definitions, requirements consistency checks, fairness testing/debugging/verification), but no empirical evaluation or concrete metric thresholds.
SOC snippet: Modern software is riddled with examples of biased behavior... bias can also emerge from ambiguous or incomplete requirement specification...
Spec snippet: (1) eliciting and specifying requirements that capture fairness properties, (2) architecting and designing systems while adhering to their fairness concerns, (3) validating and verifying fairness properties...
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Regulators, Users
Validation snippet: ...tools are needed to support software fairness testing to measure software discrimination...

Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides explicit, testable fairness constraints and an adversarial operationalization with offline evaluation across tasks, but treats fairness as a design-time model property without stakeholder elicitation, deployment monitoring, or revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Adversarial debiasing: train predictor to minimize task loss L_P while reducing adversary’s ability to infer protected variable Z from Ŷ (DemParity) or from (Ŷ,Y) (EqOdds). Update rule uses ∇_W L_P − proj_{∇_W L_P}(∇_W L_A) − α∇_W L_A. Evaluate on (i) toy scenario, (ii) word-embedding analogies (gender subspace via PCA on gender word pairs), and (iii) UCI Adult income prediction with sex as protected attribute; report confusion matrices and subgroup FPR/FNR and z-tests for differences.
Metrics used: Fairness constraints: Demographic Parity, Equality of Odds, Equality of Opportunity (Hardt et al. 2016). Uses group-wise error rates (FPR, FNR, TPR) and confusion matrices; reports achieving equality of odds within ~1% across sex groups on UCI Adult.
Notes: Focus is model-level mitigation via adversarial learning; provides theoretical guarantees under convex/concave assumptions and discusses training instability/local minima and hyperparameter scheduling (α). No deployment monitoring/revision process.
Protected attrs / groups: Protected variable Z (examples: gender; zip code as proxy). Experiments report subgroup metrics for sex (Male vs Female); discussion references race/ethnicity and gender.
Spec snippet: Defines Demographic Parity: Ŷ ⟂ Z, i.e., P(Ŷ=ŷ)=P(Ŷ=ŷ|Z=z). Defines Equality of Odds: (Ŷ ⟂ Z)|Y, i.e., P(Ŷ=ŷ|Y=y)=P(Ŷ=ŷ|Z=z,Y=y). Defines Equality of Opportunity as conditional independence on Y=y.
Threshold value: Example operational target: equality of odds achieved within 1% across protected groups (sex) on UCI Adult; no general deployment threshold policy specified.
Validation snippet: Reports empirical results on word analogies and UCI Adult; provides subgroup confusion matrices and FPR/FNR, noting debiasing yields near equality of odds with small accuracy change (86.0% vs 84.5%).

Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a general, testable operationalization of fairness requirements (DP/EO and other conditional-moment constraints) with explicit tolerance ε and strong algorithmic/finite-sample guarantees, but remains design-time and does not address stakeholder elicitation, deployment monitoring, or requirements revision over time.
Measurement procedure: Reduces fair classification (empirical constrained optimization) to repeated cost-sensitive classification via Lagrangian saddle-point game between Q-player and λ-player; solves with exponentiated gradient algorithm. Uses black-box cost-sensitive learner (e.g., weighted logistic regression, gradient-boosted trees). Finite-sample/statistical guarantees via Rademacher complexity; explores accuracy–fairness trade-offs via varying ε and (optionally) grid search for small constraint sets.
Metrics used: DP and EO constraints expressed as conditional moment (expectation) equalities/inequalities; constraint violation measured as max_a |E[h(X)|A=a] − E[h(X)]| for DP and max_{a,y} |E[h(X)|A=a,Y=y] − E[h(X)|Y=y]| for EO. Uses cost-sensitive classification reduction and returns randomized classifier Q over H minimizing empirical error under constraints.
Notes: Core contribution is an algorithmic RE-style operationalization: fairness requirements as explicit linear constraints with tolerance ε, solved via reductions to cost-sensitive classification; provides finite-sample guarantees and does not require protected attribute at test time (though experiments include A in X for fair comparison). No monitoring/revision lifecycle process.
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attribute A (pre-defined; examples include race, sex; also discusses proxy features like zipcode correlated with race). Experiments: Adult (gender; also gender+race as combined attribute), COMPAS (race: Black vs White), LSAC bar passage (race: Black vs White), Dutch census (gender).
QA notes: Uses constrained optimization (error minimization under DP/EO constraints) and reductions to cost-sensitive learning (exp-gradient saddle point) to produce randomized classifier with minimal empirical error; evaluates across multiple datasets and reports trade-off curves. Does not cover socio-technical governance or post-deployment lifecycle practices beyond offline evaluation.
Spec snippet: Defines Demographic Parity (DP): h(X) ⟂ A, i.e., P[h(X)=ŷ|A=a]=P[h(X)=ŷ] (equiv. E[h(X)|A=a]=E[h(X)]). Defines Equalized Odds (EO): h(X) ⟂ A | Y, i.e., P[h(X)=ŷ|A=a,Y=y]=P[h(X)=ŷ|Y=y] (equiv. E[h(X)|A=a,Y=y]=E[h(X)|Y=y]). Expresses fairness as linear inequalities on conditional moments Mμ(h) ≤ c.
Threshold value: Constraint slack ε used as tolerance: ε ∈ {0.001, …, 0.1}; sets ĉ_k = ε for all constraints to allow small violations. Mentions four-fifths rule (4/5) as motivation for DP.
Validation snippet: Evaluates reductions vs baselines (post-processing, reweighting, relabeling, unconstrained) on Adult, COMPAS, LSAC bar passage, and Dutch census; plots test error vs fairness-constraint violation with confidence intervals.

DES snippet: We use the theory of urns ... to analyze existing methods for predictive policing ... and demonstrate how to change the inputs to a predictive policing system (in a black-box manner) so the runaway feedback loop does not occur, allowing the true crime rate to be learned.
ENV snippet: Predictive policing systems are increasingly used to determine how to allocate police across a city ... Such systems have been empirically shown to be susceptible to runaway feedback loops ... where police are repeatedly sent back to the same neighborhoods regardless of the true crime rate.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a formal and empirically demonstrated explanation of why predictive policing can create runaway feedback loops and proposes a black-box input-filtering remedy, but does not specify fairness requirements as explicit acceptance thresholds nor provide monitoring/revision governance for maintaining fairness over time.
Measurement procedure: Model predictive policing as generalized Pólya urns with discovered vs reported incidents (weights wd, wr); prove convergence pathologies (e.g., Lemma 4: if λA>λB, asymptotic probability of sampling A tends to 1) and evaluate via simulations using Oakland/PredPol setup; apply black-box input filtering (rejection sampling) to discovered incidents so urn/PredPol converge to true crime rate ratios.
Metrics used: Deployment allocation should converge to true crime rate proportions ("Effective Policing" goal); disparity/degree of runaway feedback linked to disparity in crime rates between areas; urn model asymptotic allocation (e.g., converging to 100% for one region under discovery-only) and corrected via rejection-sampling/Thompson–Horvitz-style filtering of discovered incidents inputs.
Protected attrs / groups: Neighborhoods/regions (e.g., Oakland Top1/Top2/Random regions). No explicit protected demographic attribute is modeled; fairness concern is observational bias leading to disparate policing allocation.
QA notes: Models predictive policing as an online learning system with feedback loops; formal urn analysis proves runaway feedback ("go with the winner") under discovered-incident updates; proposes black-box input filtering (rejection sampling / Thompson–Horvitz intuition) to correct PredPol-like systems; validated via simulations using Lum & Isaac (2016) Oakland/PredPol setup. Does not frame explicit fairness requirements with demographic protected groups or acceptance thresholds, and does not cover post-deployment monitoring/revision governance beyond the corrective input policy.
SOC snippet: Decisions made by the system influence the data that is fed to it in the future ... predictive policing algorithms only observe crime in neighborhoods they patrol.
Spec snippet: Goal 3.1 (Effective Policing) A region with Λ percent of the crime in the precinct should receive Λ percent of the police.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers
Validation snippet: We develop a mathematical model ... show empirically that this model exhibits such problems ... and demonstrate how to change the inputs to a predictive policing system (in a black-box manner) ... Using the Lum and Isaac (2016) data ... we simulate ... and show PredPol converges to the appropriate crime rate predictions under the improvement policy.

DES snippet: We introduce three scalar measures of procedural fairness ... and provide fast submodular mechanisms to optimize the tradeoff between procedural fairness and prediction accuracy.
ENV snippet: We leverage the rich literature on organizational justice ... distinguish between ... distributive and procedural fairness ... humans’ moral judgments ... rooted in prevailing cultural or social norms, political beliefs, and legal regulations.
Elicitation snippet: For gathering human judgments, we use the Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) platform ... asking ... yes or no to three questions ... each feature is judged by 200 different workers.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Defines and operationalizes procedurally fair learning as explicit, testable constraints over feature sets grounded in human moral judgments and optimizes fairness–accuracy trade-offs, but does not address deployment-time monitoring or revision processes to keep procedural fairness requirements valid over time.
Measurement procedure: Collect AMT judgments (200 US-based master workers) per feature for three questions (apr./acc./disp.); compute fraction judging feature fair; define set-function fairness as fraction judging all features in subset fair with conditioning on whether feature increases accuracy/disparity; evaluate feature subsets via logistic regression (L2) on COMPAS and NYPD SQF; optimize acc(S) subject to unf(S)≤t (and converse) using constrained submodular optimization; measure outcome fairness via FPR/FNR gaps between white vs non-white.
Metrics used: Procedural/process fairness set functions over feature subsets: feature-apriori fairness(F), feature-accuracy fairness(F), feature-disparity fairness(F); disparity uses disparate mistreatment (Zafar et al. 2017a) and outcome unfairness uses |FPR_w−FPR_nw|+|FNR_w−FNR_nw| (in COMPAS experiments).
Protected attrs / groups: COMPAS recidivism: race (white vs non-white; also mentions African American vs white in survey question), sex, age. NYPD SQF: race, sex, age (plus other stop context features). Outcome disparity measured for whites vs non-whites.
QA notes: Introduces procedural/process fairness as fairness of feature usage (organizational justice lens), operationalizes via human moral judgments collected through AMT surveys, defines three explicit set-function measures (apr./acc./disp.) and proves (super/sub)modularity properties enabling constrained optimization for fairness–accuracy trade-offs. Evaluated on COMPAS and NYPD SQF with extensive feature-subset experiments; also compares to outcome fairness via FPR/FNR gaps (white vs non-white). No lifecycle monitoring/revision governance beyond offline evaluation.
SOC snippet: Our key insight ... is to rely on humans’ moral judgments ... Their collective opinions ... reflect societal consensus on the desirability of using particular features.
Spec snippet: We define the process fairness of C to be the fraction of all users who consider the use of every feature in F to be fair ... feature-apriori fairness(F) := |⋂_{f∈F} U_f| / |U|.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Users
Threshold value: ε = 5% of full range (used to decide whether a feature increases accuracy/disparity); multiple unfairness/accuracy thresholds (21 values) for constrained optimization trade-offs.
Validation snippet: We operationalize these measures on two real world datasets using human surveys on the Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) platform ... and demonstrate good performance empirically ... compare approximate solutions with brute-force optimum over feature subsets.

Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows that static fairness constraints can worsen protected-group long-term outcomes under feedback, motivating fairness requirements that explicitly model delayed impact and measurement error; does not provide concrete deployment-time monitoring or revision processes.
Measurement procedure: Model: two-group score distributions π_A, π_B over discrete score set X; success probability ρ(x); institution utility u(x); outcome Δ(x) as expected score change if selected. Evaluate fairness constraints by solving utility maximization under (i) no constraint, (ii) equal selection rate, (iii) equal TPR, then compute Δμ outcomes. Empirics: FICO TransUnion TransRisk 2003 scores (300–850), default label (≥90 days delinquency within 18–24 months), set c+=75, c−=−150; compare MaxUtil vs DemParity vs EqOpt across bank profit/loss ratios.
Metrics used: Demographic parity (equal selection rates), equality of opportunity (equal TPR), MaxUtil utility U(τ); outcome metric is change in mean score Δμ_j(τ)=Σ_x π_j(x) τ_j(x) Δ(x); outcome curve maps selection rate β→Δμ.
Notes: Key claim: static fairness constraints (DemParity/EqOpt) do not generally guarantee long-term improvement; can cause active harm in a one-step feedback model. Introduces 'outcome curve' and characterizes regimes; discusses measurement error and outcome-based alternative objective.
Protected attrs / groups: Two groups A (protected/disadvantaged) and B; experiments on race groups (Black vs White non-Hispanic) using FICO credit scores.
Spec snippet: Defines Demographic Parity as equal selection rates: Σ_x π_A(x) τ_A(x) = Σ_x π_B(x) τ_B(x). Defines Equal Opportunity as equal true positive rates: TPR_A(τ_A) = TPR_B(τ_B), where TPR_j(τ)= (Σ_x π_j(x) ρ(x) τ(x)) / (Σ_x π_j(x) ρ(x)).
Threshold value: Symbolic selection-rate thresholds: β_MaxUtil, harm threshold β0 (Δμ=0), outcome-optimal β*; plus group-dependent score thresholds (not given as fixed numbers).
Validation snippet: "We perform experiments on FICO credit score data from 2003 ... and show that under various models of bank utility and score change, the outcomes of applying fairness criteria are in line with our theoretical predictions."

DES snippet: Surveys and critiques operational fairness measures (disparate impact/demographic parity, accuracy equity, conditional accuracy, equality of opportunity, disparate mistreatment/FPR differences, counterfactual fairness) and highlights impossibility results requiring trade-offs among metrics.
ENV snippet: The paper argues that metric choices embed normative assumptions from political philosophy, and that the appropriateness of a fairness measure depends on social context (e.g., spheres of justice, luck egalitarianism, historical injustice), not just statistical properties.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Missing context assumptions, No revision process/ownership, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a cross-disciplinary (political philosophy) grounding that clarifies why fairness metrics encode contested normative commitments and must be chosen contextually, but does not supply testable acceptance thresholds, validation protocols, or monitoring/revision mechanisms to maintain fairness requirements over time.
Measurement procedure: Conceptual synthesis: maps fairness metrics and trade-offs in fair ML to debates in political philosophy (discrimination, egalitarianism, spheres of justice, luck egalitarianism, deontic justice, distributive vs representational harms). Provides implications for how to choose/justify fairness notions in context rather than prescribing a single metric.
Metrics used: Discusses/compares operational fairness definitions: disparate impact/statistical (demographic) parity; accuracy equity; conditional accuracy equity; equality of opportunity; disparate mistreatment (FPR differences); counterfactual fairness; and notes incompatibility results (Kleinberg et al. 2016).
Notes: Use as evidence that fairness requirements need environment/context modeling and explicit justification of metric choice; highlights that protected-class lists can be too narrow/static and that historical/social context (deontic justice) matters.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses protected characteristics (race, gender, religion) as salient groups in algorithmic decision-making; examples include loans, insurance, employment, recidivism prediction; also discusses representational harms (e.g., word embeddings).
QA notes: Conceptual/philosophical paper that clarifies the normative assumptions behind different fairness metrics and why metric choice is context-dependent; strong for RQ1 (cross-disciplinary translation) and framing trade-offs, but does not provide an implementable lifecycle process, thresholds, monitoring, or empirical evaluation.
SOC snippet: Emphasizes that fairness decisions involve ethical trade-offs, legitimacy of variables, and consideration of broader social harms beyond the immediate decision context, implying governance and accountability requirements for selecting and justifying metrics.
Spec snippet: Poses the core specification problem: "What does it mean for a machine learning model to be ‘fair’, in terms which can be operationalised?" and analyzes how different mathematical definitions correspond to different philosophical accounts of justice and discrimination.

DES snippet: Given a machine-learning model f ... we are interested to discover inputs I or I′ where |f(I)−f(I′)| is beyond a pre-determined threshold γ ... We call such inputs ... discriminatory inputs ... our Aeqitas approach automatically discovers discriminatory inputs ... and retrains the model to reduce its unfairness.
ENV snippet: Nondiscrimination is ... to eliminate any societal bias based on sensitive attributes, such as race, gender or religion ... it is crucial that decisions influenced by any machine-learning model are free of any unnecessary bias.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides an explicit, testable operationalization of individual fairness violation (discriminatory input pairs over sensitive attributes with a tunable discrimination threshold) and an automated testing-and-retraining pipeline, but does not address post-deployment monitoring or revision processes to keep fairness requirements valid over time.
Measurement procedure: Black-box directed test generation: global uniform sampling to find discriminatory seeds; local neighborhood search guided by robustness with random/semi-directed/fully-directed probability updates (σv, σpr) to perturb non-sensitive features while varying sensitive features exhaustively; estimate discriminatory-input prevalence via repeated uniform sampling (LLN) and compute 95% CI; retraining module iteratively augments training data with random portions of discriminatory inputs from Test and accepts updates only if estimated discrimination decreases.
Metrics used: Individual fairness violation via discriminatory input pairs differing only on sensitive parameters; discrimination magnitude |f(I)−f(I′)| compared to threshold γ; effectiveness measured as % discriminatory inputs among generated; fairness estimation via LLN; retraining effectiveness measured as reduction in estimated % discriminatory inputs (with 95% CI) and % improvement.
Protected attrs / groups: Sensitive/discriminatory parameters Pd isc include gender (used in experiments), and paper cites race, religion as examples; fairness framed as nondiscrimination w.r.t. sensitive attributes. Evaluation task: US Census income prediction (>$50k) with gender as Pd isc.
QA notes: Defines software fairness testing as finding discriminatory input pairs (individual fairness style) that differ only on sensitive parameters and yield output difference beyond threshold γ; proposes Aeqitas directed probabilistic search (global+local) leveraging model robustness to efficiently uncover discriminatory regions; provides LLN-based estimation of discriminatory-input prevalence with confidence intervals; includes automated retraining module that iteratively adds portions of discriminatory inputs to training data and accepts updates only if estimated discrimination decreases. Strong operationalization and evaluation on six classifiers; no deployment-time monitoring or requirements revision governance.
SOC snippet: Aeqitas has a completely automated module which guarantees a decrease in the percentage of discriminatory inputs in I ... to provide useful capabilities to a developer.
Spec snippet: If |f(I) − f(I′)| > γ, then I is called a discriminatory input ... manifests the violation of (individual) fairness in f.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers
Threshold value: γ discrimination threshold (user-supplied); in experiments γ=0 with Pd isc=gender; perturbation δ∈{−1,+1}; ∆v=0.001, ∆pr=0.001; termination by target # discriminatory inputs or time bound.
Validation snippet: We implemented Aeqitas and ... evaluated it on six state-of-the-art classifiers ... Aeqitas generates up to 70% discriminatory inputs ... and ... improve the fairness up to 94% ... Table 5 shows reduction in estimated % of discriminatory inputs (95% CI).

Citation: Pleiss, Geoff, Manish Raghavan, Felix Wu, Jon Kleinberg, and Kilian Q. Weinberger. "On Fairness and Calibration." In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2017.
DES snippet: Formal analysis of the incompatibility between calibration and error-rate parity constraints (Equalized Odds and relaxations). Provides geometric characterization in generalized FP/FN space, feasibility conditions, and an optimal post-processing algorithm (information withholding / randomization) to satisfy calibrated equal-cost constraints when feasible.
ENV snippet: Frames fairness concerns about predictive errors in sensitive domains (criminal justice, online advertising, medical testing) and motivates calibration as necessary for risk scores to have consistent meaning across protected groups (to avoid incentivizing decision makers to use group membership).
Failure modes: Governance absent, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides rigorous, testable results on calibration–fairness incompatibilities and an optimal (but ethically troubling) randomized post-processing for calibrated parity, yet offers no stakeholder elicitation, acceptance criteria, or deployment-time monitoring/revision guidance for choosing among incompatible fairness goals.
Measurement procedure: Given calibrated classifiers for each group, characterize feasible calibrated solutions as points on group-specific calibration lines in generalized FP/FN space. For relaxed equal-cost parity, compute interpolation parameter α and post-process by replacing h_2(x) with trivial base-rate predictor μ_2 with probability α (information withholding) so that g_2(tilde h_2)=g_1(h_1) while preserving calibration; evaluate empirically on Adult, Heart, and COMPAS datasets comparing to EO post-processing/training.
Metrics used: Calibration (within-group), generalized false-positive and false-negative rates for probabilistic classifiers, Equalized Odds (matching both error types), relaxed equal-cost parity g_t, plus evaluation via plotted FP/FN trade-off geometry; discusses base rates μ_t as key parameter.
Protected attrs / groups: Discusses group attribute defining subpopulations G1,G2 (e.g., race: African-American vs Caucasian in COMPAS; gender: women vs men in Adult; age groups: middle-aged vs seniors in Heart).
QA notes: Strong theory + clear operational definitions (calibration, generalized FP/FN, EO and relaxations) and empirical confirmation. Explicitly discusses troubling implications (random information withholding; degrading one group) and feasibility limits; still does not provide concrete stakeholder elicitation or governance/monitoring process for selecting fairness targets in practice.
SOC snippet: Highlights normative tension: group-level parity under calibration may require intentionally degrading one group’s classifier and using random coin flips to withhold information for individuals, raising individual fairness and acceptability concerns in high-stakes settings.
Spec snippet: Defines probabilistic classifiers h_t(x)∈[0,1] with generalized FP/FN rates cfp(h_t)=E[h_t(x)|y=0], cfn(h_t)=E[1−h_t(x)|y=1]; Probabilistic Equalized Odds requires matching both across groups. Group calibration requires P(y=1|h_t(x)=p)=p within each group. Shows impossibility of satisfying calibration with multiple distinct equal-cost (incl. EO) constraints unless perfect prediction; proposes calibrated relaxation via equalizing a single linear cost g_t(h_t)=a_t cfp(h_t)+b_t cfn(h_t).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Regulators
Validation snippet: Empirically confirms theoretical findings on multiple datasets (Adult income prediction, Heart health prediction, COMPAS recidivism), comparing calibrated relaxations vs Equalized Odds post-processing/training using generalized FP/FN plots.

Citation: Kilbertus, Niki, Mateo Rojas-Carulla, Giambattista Parascandolo, Moritz Hardt, Dominik Janzing, and Bernhard Schölkopf. "Avoiding Discrimination through Causal Reasoning." In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2017.
DES snippet: Introduces a causal-graph/structural-equation framework with resolving variables and proxy variables; defines causal non-discrimination notions (unresolved discrimination, proxy discrimination) and gives procedures/algorithms (notably under linearity/expression assumptions) to construct predictors satisfying these criteria.
ENV snippet: Motivates societal concern about discrimination in consequential ML decisions and critiques observational/statistical fairness criteria as insufficient to resolve fairness without assumptions about the causal data-generating process.
Failure modes: Governance absent, Missing context assumptions, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides clear, testable causal definitions and constructive procedures for avoiding certain forms of discrimination, but depends on strong causal-model assumptions and does not address stakeholder elicitation, acceptance criteria, or deployment-time monitoring/revision governance.
Measurement procedure: Assume/construct a causal graph + structural equation model (SEM). Define discrimination as path-based properties (unresolved discrimination) and interventional invariances (proxy discrimination). For linear SEMs, derive parameter constraints (e.g., choose predictor Rθ(P,X) so that distribution of R is invariant to do(P=p)), enabling learning under constraints from labeled data.
Metrics used: Causal/discrimination criteria expressed via causal graphs and interventional distributions (do-calculus), not via fixed numeric thresholds; constraints are invariance conditions under interventions on proxy P (and analogous intervention-based criteria for unresolved discrimination).
Protected attrs / groups: Protected attribute A (e.g., gender, race, religion). Discusses proxies P (e.g., name, visual features, language spoken at home) that correlate with A.
QA notes: Strong conceptual/technical framing: precisely explains why observational criteria fail (unidentifiability), proposes causal criteria (unresolved vs proxy discrimination), and gives algorithmic removal procedures (especially under linearity/expressibility assumptions). Limited empirical validation (explicitly out of scope). Stakeholder elicitation and governance/monitoring are not covered beyond abstract legal/protected-group framing; limitations include reliance on correct causal graph/SEM and functional form assumptions.
SOC snippet: Frames fairness as avoiding harmful discrimination on legally protected attributes; discusses normative subtleties (concept of protected attribute vs observable proxies) and how different assumptions lead to different fairness conclusions.
Spec snippet: Defines unresolved discrimination via existence of an unblocked directed path from protected attribute A to variable V not mediated by resolving variables; defines proxy discrimination via interventional invariance: P(R|do(P=p)) = P(R|do(P=p′)) for all p,p′ (and variants such as individual proxy discrimination).
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Regulators

Citation: Calmon, Flavio P., Dennis Wei, Bhanukiran Vinzamuri, Karthikeyan Natesan Ramamurthy, and Kush R. Varshney. "Optimized Pre-Processing for Discrimination Prevention." In Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS), 2017.
DES snippet: Formulates a probabilistic pre-processing transformation learned via (quasi)convex optimization to trade off discrimination control, utility preservation, and per-sample distortion constraints; provides pipeline (train/apply) and solver-based implementation details.
ENV snippet: Frames discrimination in algorithmic decision-making, distinguishes direct vs indirect discrimination (disparate treatment vs disparate impact), and positions the method in the ML pipeline (pre-processing) with legal/ societal context (e.g., 80% rule).
Failure modes: Governance absent, No thresholds / acceptance criteria, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides a rigorous, testable optimization framework for pre-processing to reduce group discrimination with individual distortion and utility constraints, but does not address stakeholder elicitation, deployment-time monitoring, or governance/revision processes beyond offline evaluation trade-offs.
Measurement procedure: Estimate (p_{D,X,Y}) empirically from training data, solve optimization to learn randomized mapping (p_{hat X,hat Y|X,Y,D}), transform train and test data (test uses marginalized (p_{hat X|D,X})), train standard classifiers (LR, RF), evaluate discrimination on test via max over groups of ratio-based J on model outputs and evaluate AUC via 5-fold CV.
Metrics used: Discrimination metric based on probability ratio J(p,q)=p/q−1 (motivated by the 80% rule) applied to (p_{tilde Y|D}(1|d)); utility loss via total variation distance between (p_{X,Y}) and (p_{hat X,hat Y}); prediction performance via AUC.
Protected attrs / groups: Race (African-American vs Caucasian) in COMPAS; Gender in UCI Adult (male vs female).
QA notes: Strong technical clarity and operationalization (formal constraints, convexity conditions, train/apply pipeline, empirical evaluation on COMPAS and Adult). Stakeholders are mentioned mainly as abstract legal/protected groups and practitioner choice of fairness parameters; no concrete elicitation method or artifacts. Limitations discussed around distribution mismatch / finite samples and feasibility of constraints; ethics and governance beyond the optimization are limited.
SOC snippet: Motivates fairness as non-discrimination for legally protected groups; discusses societal/legal considerations and group vs individual fairness notions and their tension/trade-offs.
Spec snippet: Defines discrimination control constraints that limit dependence of transformed outcome (hat Y) on protected variables (D) (e.g., constraints on (p_{hat Y|D}) using a ratio metric motivated by the “80% rule”), while constraining individual distortion and preserving utility via distributional closeness.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Regulators
Validation snippet: Benchmarks the learned pre-processing transformations on COMPAS recidivism and UCI Adult datasets, training LR/RF on transformed data and reporting discrimination–AUC trade-off curves; compares to baselines (original, dropping D, LFR).

DES snippet: Proposes random classifier ensembles: an ensemble Cens selects one classifier Ck at random per case according to p(j). Shows formal properties: ensembles preserve equality of treatment; preserve equality of impact for acceptance-rate and TPR/TNR definitions; may fail for predictive value definitions; and ensembles of unfair classifiers can still meet parity constraints and improve accuracy–fairness trade-offs.
ENV snippet: Frames a socio-technical risk of replacing diverse human decision makers with a single scalable algorithm: loss of decision-process diversity. Motivates randomness/assignment analogies from courts (random judge panels) as an ENV-level fairness condition (equal chance of being judged by different ‘schools of thought’).
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Gap summary (1 sentence): Shows that randomness via classifier ensembles can preserve or even improve standard parity-based fairness and accuracy–fairness trade-offs and motivates distributional (intra-group) fairness requirements, but provides no lifecycle monitoring or revision mechanisms to maintain these fairness properties under drift or changing contexts.
Measurement procedure: Construct an ensemble of classifiers {Cj} with sampling distribution p(j); for a user x, sample a classifier then output its decision. Analyze fairness via expectations over the ensemble randomness; show when linearity of expectation preserves impact parity. Provide synthetic examples where unfair classifiers combined stochastically meet fairness constraints and improve accuracy–fairness trade-offs versus single constrained classifier. (Evaluation details beyond examples not fully specified in excerpt.)
Metrics used: Fairness metrics discussed: equality of treatment; equality of impact measured by acceptance rate into positive/negative class; TPR/TNR; predictive positive/negative value (PPV/NPV) with caveat. New distributional fairness (intra-group): whether beneficial outcome probabilities are evenly distributed within a sensitive group (e.g., all members have equal chance), not only equal group rates.
Protected attrs / groups: Assumes one binary sensitive attribute z ∈ {0,1} (e.g., gender or race). Examples use men vs women; mentions race in motivating domains (COMPAS).
QA notes: Highlights a RE-relevant requirement gap: algorithmization reduces decision-process diversity. Proposes random ensembles as a design mechanism with formal fairness properties across several parity metrics, and introduces distributional fairness (intra-group equal chance) as a new requirement notion enabled by randomness. Evidence is primarily analytical + illustrative synthetic examples; no deployment monitoring/revision/governance process described.
SOC snippet: Discusses operational implications of randomness: reduced gaming (‘fence around the law’), smoother expected outcomes near boundaries, and motivates new “distributional fairness” notions capturing intra-group fairness (even distribution of beneficial-outcome chance within a group), beyond standard inter-group parity metrics. No governance/monitoring plan is specified.
Spec snippet: Defines ensemble classifier Cens: select Ck from {Cj} with probability p(j), output Cens(x)=Ck(x). Equality of treatment: prediction independent of sensitive attribute. Equality of impact: equal beneficial-outcome rates across groups for acceptance-rate or TPR/TNR definitions; not guaranteed for PPV/NPV since conditioning sets vary across Cj. Introduces distributional fairness idea: within-group equality of chance at beneficial outcomes.
Stakeholders Mentioned: Affected groups, Developers, Org leadership, Other, Regulators
Threshold value: No fixed numeric thresholds; fairness is expressed as parity equalities on expected rates (and distributional fairness as equal probabilities within group).
Validation snippet: Uses constructed synthetic decision scenarios to demonstrate three properties (fairness preservation, unfair-to-fair ensembles, and improved accuracy–fairness trade-offs) and motivates distributional fairness via illustrative examples.

DES snippet: Reformulates fairness as constrained optimization: maximize immediate utility (public safety benefit minus detention cost) subject to formal fairness constraints (statistical parity, conditional statistical parity, predictive equality). Proves optimal constrained rules are deterministic threshold rules on risk p(Y=1|X) with group-specific thresholds t_g (and for conditional parity, thresholds depend on group + legitimate factors). Unconstrained optimum is a single uniform threshold c for all individuals.
ENV snippet: Frames the deployment context as pretrial release decisions where algorithmic risk tools can create racial disparities; also surfaces a competing normative/legal notion of equality: holding all individuals to the same standard irrespective of race, contrasted with group-parity definitions that imply race-specific treatment.
Failure modes: Drift not addressed, Governance absent, No revision process/ownership, Other
Fairness notions: Contextual/legal compliance, Error parity, Other, Outcome parity
Gap summary (1 sentence): Provides explicit, testable formulations of common fairness constraints and proves utility-optimal (often race-specific) threshold decision rules, empirically quantifying substantial public-safety trade-offs on Broward County data, but does not address post-deployment monitoring or revision of fairness requirements under shifting data or governance contexts.
Measurement procedure: Model decisions as threshold rules over estimated risk p(Y=1|X) (learned via L1-regularized logistic regression + Platt scaling on Broward County data, excluding race). For each fairness constraint, choose group-specific thresholds to (i) satisfy the constraint, (ii) detain a fixed fraction (e.g., 30%), and (iii) maximize expected public safety/utility; evaluate via repeated train-test splits (e.g., 100 random 70/30 splits) and report impact on violent recidivism and proportion of detained low-risk individuals.
Metrics used: Fairness constraints: statistical parity (equal detention rates), conditional statistical parity (equal detention rates conditional on ‘legitimate’ factors), predictive equality (equal FPR). Also discusses calibration for risk scores. Utility metric: immediate utility u(d,c)=E[Y d(X) − c d(X)] with Y indicating benefit of detention (e.g., would recidivate if released).
Protected attrs / groups: Race groups (black vs white) in Broward County COMPAS dataset; notes logic applies to other protected attributes (e.g., gender).
QA notes: Strong formalization of fairness requirements as explicit constraints and utility objective; derives optimal threshold-based decision rules and shows constrained fairness can require race-specific thresholds (raising normative/legal concerns). Provides offline empirical quantification of cost-of-fairness on Broward County data with repeated splits. Does not address lifecycle monitoring/revision; acknowledges outcome-data bias, subgroup validity, and long-term utility limits.
SOC snippet: Discusses policy and governance implications: constrained rules require explicit race-specific thresholds which may raise strict scrutiny under Equal Protection; highlights that fairness constraints can detain low-risk individuals and release higher-risk ones, and emphasizes that algorithm choice is only one intervention among others (services, supervision, discretion). Does not specify monitoring/revision processes.
Spec snippet: Defines decision rule d: R^p → [0,1] (probability of detain). Statistical parity: E[d(X)|g(X)] = E[d(X)] (Eq.1). Conditional statistical parity: E[d(X)|ℓ(X),g(X)] = E[d(X)|ℓ(X)] (Eq.2).
